PDF-0582.title: Introverts and extraverts choose different noise levels — and each performs best at their self-selected intensity
PDF-0582.text:
Given the choice, introverts select quieter environments than extraverts — and each personality type performs best at the intensity they chose. Russell Geen demonstrated this elegant interaction in a 1984 study in the *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology*, providing some of the strongest evidence that the optimal acoustic environment depends on the person, not just the decibel level.

The experiment used a between-subjects design crossed with an individual-difference variable. Participants classified as introverts or extraverts on the Eysenck Personality Inventory were asked to select a "comfortable" noise level for a learning task (paired-associate recall). They adjusted a white noise generator to their preferred intensity. Introverts chose significantly lower levels than extraverts. Then, crucially, participants were tested either at their self-selected noise level or at the level selected by the other personality group (introverts received extraverts' preferred noise, and vice versa).

The results produced a crossover interaction. Introverts performed best at their own (lower) noise level and poorly at the extraverts' (higher) noise level. Extraverts performed best at their own (higher) noise level and poorly at the introverts' (lower) noise level. Each personality type suffered when forced into the other's preferred acoustic environment. Physiological arousal (skin conductance) confirmed the pattern: each group showed moderate arousal at their preferred level and over- or under-arousal at the mismatched level.

Geen interpreted the findings through Eysenck's arousal theory: introverts have higher baseline cortical arousal and therefore require less external stimulation to reach optimal performance; extraverts have lower baseline arousal and seek more stimulation. When the environment matches the individual's optimal stimulation level, performance is maximal. When it mismatches — too much for introverts, too little for extraverts — performance degrades.

For workspace designers, the implication challenges the one-size-fits-all approach to acoustic design. An open-plan office set to a single noise level — whether quiet or moderately noisy — will be suboptimal for a significant portion of occupants. Providing acoustic variety within a workplace (quiet zones for introverts, moderately stimulating collaborative zones for extraverts, and individual control over personal sound environments) would better match the range of arousal needs in a typical workforce.

The finding also explains why debates about "ideal office noise levels" are unresolvable at the population level. There is no single optimum — the optimum varies with personality. Standards that specify maximum allowable noise levels protect against extreme conditions but cannot optimize for individual differences. Individual acoustic control — through personal sound masking, noise-canceling headphones, or spatial zoning by activity type — is the design strategy that accommodates the introvert-extravert spectrum.

The study is from 1984, with a modest sample size, and uses a laboratory paradigm (paired-associate learning) that may not generalize to complex workplace tasks. The introvert-extravert distinction captures one axis of individual variation; noise sensitivity, attention deficit traits, and cultural norms also modulate optimal stimulation levels. The white noise used in the study is less disruptive than the speech-containing noise typical of real offices.

What field experiment — providing open-plan office workers with individually adjustable sound masking systems and personality-assessed target levels, while measuring productivity across six months — could test whether personality-matched acoustic environments improve performance compared to fixed acoustic conditions?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0582.pdf

PDF-0583.title: PDF-0583
PDF-0583.text:
Put a mouse in a box with two exits, one white and one black. Shock it every time it enters the black exit. The mouse learns to choose white. Now vary the shock strength: weak, medium, or strong. Robert Yerkes and John Dodson at Harvard showed in this 1908 study in the Journal of Comparative Neurology and Psychology that medium-strength stimulation produced the fastest learning — not weak, not strong, but an intermediate level. This inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance became one of the most cited principles in all of psychology.

The puzzle that drove the study was practical. Yerkes was training dancing mice to discriminate black from white passageways. He needed to know which shock level would train them fastest. Too little shock and the mouse had no reason to avoid the wrong box. Too much shock and the mouse panicked, choosing randomly or freezing. The question was whether an optimal middle existed.

The experiments used three sets of conditions. Set I tested medium difficulty (the two exits differed moderately in brightness) at weak, medium, and strong shock. Set II tested an easy discrimination (large brightness difference) at five shock levels. Set III tested a difficult discrimination (small brightness difference) at four shock levels.

Table 2 (p. 5) shows the training data: the number of mice, their age at training start, and the stimulus strength for each condition. Mice were six to eight weeks old. Each mouse ran repeated trials until it chose the white exit on ten consecutive attempts.

For the medium discrimination (Set I), mice trained with medium shock learned fastest. Mice trained with weak shock learned slowest. Mice trained with strong shock learned at an intermediate rate. For the easy discrimination (Set II), stronger shock still helped: the strongest stimulus produced the fastest learning because the task was easy enough that panic did not interfere. For the difficult discrimination (Set III), strong shock impaired learning severely. Mice shocked heavily on a hard task took far longer to learn than mice shocked lightly.

The interaction is the key finding: the optimal arousal level depends on task difficulty. Easy tasks benefit from high arousal. Hard tasks benefit from low arousal. This is the Yerkes-Dodson Law. The five figures in the paper plot trials-to-criterion against stimulus strength for each discrimination level, showing the inverted-U curve most clearly for the medium and difficult tasks.

For architects and environmental designers, the law explains why high-stimulation environments (loud music, bright flashing lights, complex visual fields) help people perform simple physical tasks but impair complex cognitive work. A trading floor benefits from high arousal. A library needs low arousal. A design studio working on difficult creative problems needs moderate stimulation — enough to stay alert, not enough to overwhelm.

The study used dancing mice, not humans. The stimulus was painful electric shock, not the ambient sensory stimulation that characterizes buildings. The discrimination task was a binary choice, far simpler than real-world cognitive work. The number of mice per condition varied and was not always reported. Despite these limits, the inverted-U principle replicated across hundreds of human studies over the following century.

Does the Yerkes-Dodson curve apply to creative design work in real offices? A study crossing three ambient noise levels with two task difficulties (routine drafting versus novel design) while measuring both cortisol and design quality ratings would test whether the inverted-U holds for architectural cognition.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0583.pdf

PDF-0589.title: Floor plan shape predicts place attachment: Shorter plans feel more like home
PDF-0589.text:
The shape of your apartment predicts how attached you feel to it. This study investigated whether floor plan geometry — the proportional relationship between the length and width of a dwelling's plan — affects place attachment, finding that shorter, more compact plans produced significantly higher attachment ratings than elongated plans. The result connects an apparently abstract geometric property to one of the deepest emotional relationships between a person and their environment.

Floor plan shape, specifically the ratio of plan depth to plan width, was operationalized as short versus long. Short plans have a width-to-depth ratio closer to 1:1 (approaching square), while long plans are narrow and deep, with corridors connecting rooms arranged in sequence. The manipulation isolated plan geometry from other variables — both conditions had equivalent floor area, room count, and fenestration — so the observed difference in place attachment is attributable to the spatial proportions, not to size or amenity.

The significant effect of plan shape on place attachment is architecturally consequential because place attachment is not a trivial aesthetic preference but a deep psychological construct. Place attachment encompasses emotional bonding with a location, identity investment in the home, behavioral commitment to staying, and social rootedness in a neighborhood community. People with high place attachment are more likely to maintain their dwellings, invest in their neighborhoods, report higher life satisfaction, and experience lower psychological distress. A design variable that increases place attachment is not merely making a nicer apartment — it is strengthening the occupant's psychological investment in their home and community.

Why would shorter plans produce more attachment? The study does not directly test mechanisms, but several hypotheses align with established environmental psychology. First, spatial integration: shorter plans place rooms in closer proximity, creating a more integrated domestic environment where family members can hear, see, and sense each other's presence across the dwelling. A compact plan creates a sense of spatial togetherness that an elongated plan — where the bedroom may be 15 meters from the kitchen along a corridor — does not. Second, spatial legibility: shorter plans are easier to mentally map because the spatial relationships between rooms are simpler and more visible. From the central living area of a compact plan, the occupant can orient to all other rooms. In an elongated plan, rooms at the far end may be invisible and psychologically remote. Third, enclosure quality: shorter plans have lower surface-to-area ratios, which means less exterior wall per unit of floor area. This lower ratio produces more interior rooms, greater thermal stability, and a more enveloped, cocoon-like spatial quality that may feel more sheltering and more domestic.

The finding has direct implications for residential building design, particularly for the apartment blocks and housing towers that accommodate millions of urban residents worldwide. Developer-driven floor plate design often produces elongated plans driven by the economic logic of maximizing units per floor: narrow frontages minimize the building's street dimension, and deep plans extend units toward the building core at minimal structural cost. The result is apartments where the kitchen and bedroom are connected by a corridor rather than a spatial relationship — apartments that function as efficient containers but feel like corridors with rooms attached rather than homes.

The compact plan alternative sacrifices some units-per-floor efficiency for spatial quality. Wider frontages with shallower plans create apartments where rooms relate to each other spatially — the kitchen opens to the living room, which opens to the bedroom zone, with each room aware of the others. The occupant lives in a space, not along a corridor. If this spatial quality produces measurably higher place attachment, the developer's calculus should include the downstream benefits: higher tenant retention (lower turnover costs), better maintenance behavior (lower maintenance costs), and higher resale or rental value (demonstrated through hedonic pricing models that link plan quality to market value).

For architects designing housing, the finding prescribes a plan geometry target. Minimize the length-to-width ratio of dwelling plans. When building depth and structural constraints force elongated plans, mitigate the corridor effect through internal spatial connections (open kitchens, visual connections between rooms, internal windows, split-level sections that allow rooms to relate vertically when they cannot relate horizontally).

The study tested floor plan shape through image-based evaluation, not through lived experience in actual dwellings. Whether the place attachment difference observed in plan image ratings translates to actual attachment in occupied dwellings requires post-occupancy validation. The study controlled for floor area but the interaction between plan shape and dwelling size may produce different attachment patterns at different scales.

Would a post-occupancy study — measuring place attachment scores in matched pairs of apartments with short versus long floor plans, held constant for area, location, and amenity, assessed at 6 months and 2 years of occupancy — confirm that compact plan geometry produces measurably stronger place attachment in lived experience?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0589.pdf

PDF-0590.title: High ceilings prime freedom thinking — low ceilings prime detail focus
PDF-0590.text:
The height of the ceiling above your head changes how you think — not what you think about, but the type of cognitive processing you deploy. Joan Meyers-Levy and Rui Zhu demonstrated this priming effect in three experiments published in *Journal of Consumer Research* in 2007, showing that high ceilings (10 feet) activate freedom-related concepts and relational processing, while low ceilings (8 feet) activate confinement-related concepts and item-specific, detail-oriented processing.

The mechanism is conceptual priming. In Experiment 1, participants in a room with a 10-foot ceiling generated more freedom-related words on an ambiguous word completion task than participants in a room with an 8-foot ceiling, who generated more confinement-related words. The ceiling height did not change what participants were asked to do — it changed the associative network that was activated in the background, shaping which concepts came to mind more easily.

Experiments 2 and 3 tested the cognitive consequences. Relational processing — the ability to identify commonalities and connections across items — was enhanced under high ceilings. Item-specific processing — attention to unique distinguishing details — was enhanced under low ceilings. This was not a preference effect but a processing effect: participants were equally accurate in both rooms, but the type of processing they spontaneously engaged differed.

The design implication is striking. A design studio where architects brainstorm spatial concepts should have high ceilings to promote relational, abstract thinking. An accounting office where analysts check numbers should have lower ceilings to promote focused, detail-oriented processing. A hospital diagnostic room where physicians must both generate differential diagnoses (relational) and check specific test values (item-specific) would ideally transition between ceiling heights — or provide both types of space for different phases of the diagnostic process.

The finding extends the broader evidence that spatial properties of the built environment affect cognition below the level of conscious awareness. Participants did not notice or report the ceiling height as relevant to their performance. The priming occurred automatically, through the evolved association between overhead space and behavioral freedom versus confinement. This is not a metaphorical claim — the brain processes the physical absence of constraint overhead as a cue that maps onto conceptual freedom.

The three experiments used controlled laboratory environments, not real offices or classrooms. The ceiling height manipulation (10 ft vs. 8 ft) was confounded with room volume (both rooms had the same floor area), meaning that participants' sense of room size may have contributed to the effect. The participant samples were US undergraduates, and it is unknown whether the freedom-confinement priming holds in cultures where spatial metaphors for freedom differ. The effect sizes, while statistically significant, were modest — ceiling height shifts the probability distribution of cognitive processing type, it does not determine it.

What within-subjects experiment — having the same participants solve both creative (relational) and analytical (item-specific) tasks in both high- and low-ceiling VR rooms, with order counterbalanced — could test whether ceiling height produces a full crossover interaction between space and task type?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0590.pdf

PDF-0591.title: PDF-0591
PDF-0591.text:
What color should a classroom ceiling be — and how high? Sieun Kim and Nayeon Kim at Yonsei University showed in this 2025 study in Building and Environment that both color and ceiling height shift brain activity and perceived comfort in learning environments, and that the two interact.

Classroom design often defaults to white walls and standard ceiling heights. But color and proportion are sensory signals that the brain processes continuously. Do they affect learning-related brain states?

Forty undergraduates experienced immersive VR learning environments with six color schemes (red, green, blue hues × high-value low-chroma vs. low-value high-chroma) and four ceiling heights (3 to 6 m). EEG measured relative alpha power (an index of relaxation). Blue with low-value high-chroma produced the highest alpha power, indicating the greatest neural relaxation. Blue with high-value low-chroma received the most positive psychological ratings. Ceiling height effects peaked at 5 m for alpha power but at 4 m for perceived comfort. A 4- to 5-m ceiling may offer the best balance between neural calm and subjective satisfaction.

For school and university designers, the study offers specific targets: use blue-toned environments for classrooms aimed at focused, calm work. Set ceiling heights between 4 and 5 m when feasible, avoiding both the compression of low ceilings and the exposure of very high ones.

The study used VR, not real classrooms. The sample was young adults only.

Would a semester-long field study comparing classrooms painted in the optimal blue scheme with 4.5-m ceilings versus standard white classrooms — tracking exam scores, stress surveys, and EEG during lectures — show that color-and-height optimization improves real academic outcomes? A semester-long field comparison would test the VR predictions.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0591.pdf

PDF-0593.title: Color effects on environmental compatibility: How surface color modulates perceived fit between objects and spaces
PDF-0593.text:
Color changes how things belong. This study investigated whether color has a significant effect on evaluations of compatibility — the perceived fit between objects and their spatial context — finding a significant main effect (F-test significant) that confirms color is not merely a surface property but a relational property that modulates how well elements are perceived to belong together in an environment.

Compatibility is a construct from environmental psychology that captures the coherence between an element and its surroundings. A wooden bench in a park feels compatible — the material, form, and color belong to the setting. The same bench in a hospital corridor feels incompatible — it belongs elsewhere. Compatibility is not merely aesthetic preference but a cognitive assessment of environmental coherence that affects comfort, orientation, and the sense that an environment "makes sense."

The study manipulated the color of objects within interior environments and measured participants' compatibility ratings. The finding that color significantly affects compatibility means that changing the color of an element — without altering its form, material, texture, or position — changes how well it is perceived to fit its spatial context. A chair that looks compatible in blue may look incompatible in red, not because red is disliked but because the color relationship between the chair and its surroundings is disrupted.

The color-compatibility relationship operates through at least three mechanisms. First, color harmony: colors that share hue families, complementary relationships, or analogous positions on the color wheel are perceived as more harmonious, and harmonic color relationships produce higher compatibility ratings. A green chair in a room with warm wood tones and earth-colored textiles may produce lower compatibility than a brown chair, because green disrupts the warm color family that unifies the other elements.

Second, color congruence with material expectations: materials carry expected color ranges. Wood is expected to be brown, tan, or amber. Stone is expected to be grey, beige, or warm cream. Metal is expected to be silver, black, or bronze. When a surface is colored outside its expected material range — green wood, pink stone, yellow metal — the color violates material identity expectations, producing reduced compatibility even if the color is aesthetically pleasant in isolation.

Third, color associations with spatial function: different spatial functions carry color expectations that cultural convention has established. Healthcare spaces are expected to be white, blue, or green (cleanliness, calm, healing). Educational spaces are expected to be moderate in color intensity (focus without distraction). Hospitality spaces are expected to be warm and saturated (welcome, comfort, indulgence). When the color of an element violates the functional color expectation of the space, compatibility decreases.

For architects and interior designers, the compatibility finding reframes color specification from an isolated aesthetic decision to a relational design decision. The question is not "what color should this chair be?" but "what color should this chair be in this room, with these walls, this floor, this light, and this program?" The same color that produces high compatibility in one spatial context may produce low compatibility in another. Color specification must be contextual — evaluated within the complete material and chromatic environment rather than in isolation.

The practical response is contextual color evaluation. Rather than selecting colors from isolated swatches, designers should evaluate color choices within the full spatial context — either through physical mock-ups that place the colored element in the actual or simulated room, or through digital renderings that represent the complete color environment. VR environments that allow real-time color changes to individual elements within a complete scene provide the ideal evaluation context: the designer can adjust the color of a single element and immediately assess its compatibility with the surrounding palette.

The compatibility construct also connects to long-term occupant satisfaction. An environment where all elements feel compatible — where colors, materials, and forms belong together — produces a sense of environmental coherence that supports comfort and reduces cognitive effort. An environment where elements feel incompatible — where color relationships are discordant, where materials clash, where objects seem to belong elsewhere — produces a low-level cognitive irritation that accumulates over time. The compatible environment feels effortless; the incompatible environment feels slightly wrong, even if the occupant cannot articulate why.

The study measured compatibility through subjective ratings in response to images. Whether the color-compatibility effect holds at the same magnitude when participants experience real rooms with real colored objects — where tactile, thermal, and olfactory information from materials supplements visual color information — is not tested. The specific color relationships that maximize compatibility across different spatial types are not mapped.

Would a parametric study — systematically varying the color of individual elements within interior environments across multiple spatial types (office, healthcare, residential, retail) while measuring compatibility, coherence, and satisfaction — produce a color-compatibility matrix that architects can use for evidence-based color specification?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0593.pdf

PDF-0594.title: PDF-0594
PDF-0594.text:
Which indoor design elements help you concentrate, and which spark creativity? Ze-Yu Wang, Ji Young Cho, and Yi-Kyung Hong at Kyung Hee University showed in this 2024 study in Sensors that rectilinear shapes and red walls boost EEG markers of focused attention, while curvilinear layouts with nature views increase theta waves linked to creative thinking.

Indoor environments affect mental states, but most studies rely on self-report. Wang, Cho, and Hong used EEG to measure brain responses directly while participants viewed 16 images combining four design elements: room shape, furniture layout, ceiling height, and color.

EEG data from 20 participants showed that brainwave activity concentrated in parietal and occipital lobes rather than frontal regions. Rectilinear spaces with traditional furniture and red walls produced the highest concentration index. Curvilinear spaces with collaborative furniture, white walls, and a full nature view produced the highest relative theta, the EEG signature for creative thinking. Brain and subjective responses agreed on layout and shape but diverged on color.

For interior designers, the study suggests: design focus rooms with straight lines and warm color accents; design brainstorming rooms with curves, open layouts, and nature views.

The study used static images, not real rooms, and a small sample.

Would a real-room study — measuring EEG, task performance, and creative output in offices designed for focus versus creativity — confirm that shape and color drive distinct cognitive states? A real-room productivity study would test the design prescriptions.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0594.pdf

PDF-0595.title: Ceiling height and enclosure activate dissociable neural circuits for beauty and escape
PDF-0595.authors: Vartanian, Navarrete, Chatterjee, Fich, Gonzalez-Mora, Leder, Modroño, Nadal, Rostrup, Skov
PDF-0595.text:
Rooms with high ceilings are judged more beautiful than rooms with low ceilings. Open rooms are judged more beautiful — and more inviting to enter — than enclosed rooms. And the neural mechanisms driving these two judgments are different. Oshin Vartanian, Gorka Navarrete, Anjan Chatterjee, Lars Brorson Fich, and colleagues demonstrated all three findings in a 2015 fMRI study published in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* — one of the landmark papers in neuroarchitecture.

The experimental logic was clean. Participants viewed images of rooms varying along three dimensions — ceiling height (high vs. low), perceived enclosure (open vs. enclosed), and contour (rectilinear vs. curvilinear) — producing a 2×2×2 factorial design. On each trial, participants made one of two binary judgments: "Is this room beautiful?" or "Would you enter or exit this room?" The fMRI data captured which brain regions tracked each architectural dimension during each judgment type.

Behaviorally, ceiling height significantly affected beauty judgments (Wilcoxon Z = −1.76, p = .039, r = .41): rooms with high ceilings were more likely to be judged beautiful. Perceived enclosure affected both beauty (Z = −3.27, p = .001, r = .77) and approach-avoidance decisions (Z = −3.51, p = .001, r = .83): open rooms were rated more beautiful and more likely to be entered. The effect sizes for enclosure were large — an r of .83 means that enclosure alone explained roughly 69% of the variance in exit decisions.

The fMRI results revealed the dissociation. For beauty judgments, the high-minus-low ceiling contrast activated left precuneus (BA 19, T = 4.08) and left middle frontal gyrus (BA 6, T = 4.02) — regions involved in visuospatial exploration and attention allocation. These activations suggest that higher ceilings engage upward visual scanning and spatial mapping, and this exploratory engagement registers as aesthetic pleasure. The open-minus-enclosed contrast, by contrast, activated left middle temporal gyrus (BA 21, T = 4.17) and right superior temporal gyrus (BA 22, T = 4.11) — part of the dorsal stream circuitry that processes perceived visual and locomotive permeability. Open rooms activate the brain's motion-planning systems, and that sense of potential movement feeds beauty.

For approach-avoidance decisions, ceiling height had no significant neural effect — high ceilings make a room feel beautiful but do not change whether you want to stay. Enclosure, however, produced a striking finding: enclosed rooms activated the anterior midcingulate cortex (aMCC, BA 24) with a large cluster (T = 6.63, d = 1.56, k = 774 voxels). The aMCC receives direct projections from the amygdala. This activation pattern suggests that reduced visual and locomotive permeability — the defining quality of enclosed spaces — triggers an emotional response, possibly threat-related, that drives exit decisions below the threshold of conscious deliberation.

This dissociation carries a direct design implication. Ceiling height and enclosure affect different neural systems. The beauty of a high ceiling and the beauty of an open room are not the same kind of beauty, neurally speaking. An architect who raises ceilings engages visuospatial exploration circuitry. An architect who removes walls engages motion-planning and emotional-regulation circuitry. The two design moves are neurally independent — and their effects on whether someone wants to remain in a space are independent too.

The study has important constraints. Participants viewed static images while lying supine in a scanner — not walking through rooms. The experience of architectural enclosure involves proprioception, movement, and the full visual field in ways that 2D images cannot capture. The contour manipulation (rectilinear vs. curvilinear) was included in the design but was not the focus of the analysis. Participant demographics were only partially reported. And the study cannot distinguish whether the aMCC activation for enclosed spaces reflects a conscious aesthetic judgment or an automatic threat-detection response.

What follow-up VR study — varying ceiling height and enclosure independently while measuring both fMRI and physiological stress markers like salivary cortisol and skin conductance — could determine whether the aMCC activation for enclosed spaces reflects deliberate evaluation or an automatic alarm?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0595.pdf

PDF-0596.title: PDF-0596
PDF-0596.text:
Make the classroom bigger. Raise the ceiling. Do students learn more? Yalin Zhang, Chao Liu, Jiaxin Li, Xiaotong Jing, Jing Shi, and Weijun Gao at Qingdao University of Technology and Kitakyushu University showed in this 2024 study in Scientific Reports that classroom size alone does not improve task performance — but ceiling height does, and the effect shows in both brain waves and heart rate.

Architects debate whether larger classrooms help students or just waste space. Previous studies disagreed: some found that bigger rooms reduced crowding stress, others found no effect on grades. Nobody had tested size and ceiling height independently while measuring both behavior and physiology in the same experiment.

The team built four virtual classrooms in VR: small (40.5 m2) with a low ceiling (3.0 m), small with a high ceiling (3.9 m), large (62.1 m2) with a low ceiling (3.9 m), and large with a high ceiling (4.8 m). Thirty-four students completed cognitive tasks in each room while wearing EEG caps and heart rate monitors. They also rated each room on comfort and satisfaction.

Classroom size did not significantly affect task scores. But ceiling height did. Task scores improved by 17.3% in the big-and-high room compared to the big-and-low room, and by 20.1% in the small-and-low room compared to — counterintuitively — an even lower baseline. EEG showed that higher ceilings increased beta power (active thinking) by 26.5% in the large high-ceiling room and by 53.9% in the small high-ceiling room. Heart rate variability also shifted: the HRV-nLF/nHF ratio (a marker of autonomic balance) increased by 10.7% and 22.8% with ceiling height.

Subjective ratings tracked the physiology: students rated larger rooms as more comfortable, but the performance gain came from ceiling height, not floor area.

For school architects, the take-home is precise: invest in ceiling height first, floor area second. A small classroom with a high ceiling may outperform a large classroom with a low ceiling on both comfort and cognitive engagement.

The study used VR, not real classrooms. Thirty-four students is a modest sample. The tasks lasted minutes, not the hours of a real school day. The VR rooms lacked thermal, acoustic, and olfactory variation.

Would a semester-long comparison of two matched real classrooms — same floor area, one with a 3.0 m ceiling and one with a 4.5 m ceiling — show higher standardized test scores, lower teacher-reported off-task behavior, and higher student satisfaction in the tall room? A real-building quasi-experiment would test whether the VR ceiling-height advantage transfers to daily learning.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0596.pdf

PDF-0599.title: Interaction of window glazing type and dynamic LED lighting on daylight quality, alertness, and task performance
PDF-0599.text:
Can the right window glass and the right LED make office workers more alert? The answer depends on whether the two light sources work together or against each other. Every window filters the sun's spectrum before it reaches the occupant — clear glass transmits broadly, low-emissivity glass blocks infrared, tinted glass strips out short wavelengths that the circadian system needs, and photovoltaic glass can shift the entire indoor spectrum toward blue. Meanwhile, the LED luminaires on the ceiling provide their own spectrum, which may complement or contradict whatever the window lets through. Fatma Büşra Köse at the İzmir Institute of Technology investigated this interaction in a 2024 doctoral dissertation, combining daylight simulation with a controlled human study to determine how window glazing type and dynamic LED lighting jointly shape alertness and task performance.

The study examined multiple glazing types — clear, low-emissivity (low-e), tinted, and photovoltaic (PV) glass — and characterized their dramatically different effects on indoor spectral distribution. Clear glass transmitted the broadest spectrum, preserving the short-wavelength content that drives melanopic stimulation through the intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells. Low-e glass blocked infrared while partially attenuating short wavelengths. Tinted glass significantly filtered blue content, reducing melanopic equivalent daylight illuminance (M-EDI) at the occupant's eye. Blue photovoltaic glass (designated G8 in the study) intensified cooler blue tones at 500 nm, potentially enhancing melanopic stimulation but altering color rendering in ways that some occupants found unnatural.

Dynamic LED lighting — tunable white luminaires that shifted CCT across the day, warmer in the morning, cooler at midday, warmer again in the afternoon — was tested in combination with each glazing type. The comparison condition was static LED lighting at fixed CCT and illuminance. The critical finding: dynamic LED lighting combined with daylight through appropriate glazing improved occupant alertness compared to static LED lighting alone. The circadian-aligned LED program compensated for the spectral losses imposed by tinted or low-e glazing, effectively restoring the melanopic content that the glazing had filtered out. When glazing and LED worked in concert — a glazing type that transmitted adequate short-wavelength daylight paired with a dynamic LED that tracked the circadian curve — occupants performed better on tasks requiring sustained attention than under any static lighting condition.

The study measured positive affect as a function of melanopic stimulation intensity. Positive affect increased significantly when circadian stimulus (CS) exceeded 0.3 (p = 0.00) and when equivalent melanopic lux (EML) exceeded 250 (p = 0.01). Below these thresholds, affect was neutral or slightly negative regardless of photopic illuminance — demonstrating that the critical variable for mood is not how much light reaches the eye, but how much light reaches the melanopsin photoreceptors specifically. Work performance improvements appeared especially on tasks requiring sustained attention, where the dynamic lighting condition produced measurably fewer lapses and faster response times.

The interaction between glazing and LED type generated specific failure modes that the dissertation documented. A high-performance tinted glazing that architects specify for solar heat gain control may strip so much short-wavelength content that even a well-calibrated dynamic LED cannot fully compensate — the combined indoor spectrum still falls below the CS > 0.3 threshold during morning hours when circadian entrainment is most important. Conversely, photovoltaic glass that intensifies blue wavelengths may push melanopic stimulation above comfortable levels when combined with cool-white LEDs, producing a spectrum that feels harsh and over-stimulating. The dissertation argues that these failure modes are predictable and preventable if glazing and electric lighting are specified as a coordinated spectral system rather than independently.

For architects and lighting designers, the practical prescription is concrete. Specify glazing and electric lighting as a coordinated system, not independently. When selecting a high-performance glazing for thermal control, calculate its spectral transmittance in the melanopic band (approximately 480 nm peak). Then specify a supplementary LED system whose spectral power distribution compensates for the glazing's melanopic losses. Use dynamic (time-programmed) LED rather than static, because the circadian system requires different spectral input at different times of day. Verify that the combined daylight-plus-LED spectrum achieves CS > 0.3 and EML > 250 at the occupant's eye during morning hours. A designer who specifies a beautiful bronze-tinted curtain wall without calculating its melanopic transmittance may produce an office that looks elegant, meets thermal targets, and chronobiologically starves every worker inside it.

The study was conducted in controlled laboratory conditions, not occupied offices. Exposure durations were short (hours per session, not weeks of sustained occupancy). Individual differences in light sensitivity, chronotype, and age-related lens yellowing were not modeled as covariates. The photovoltaic glazing conditions raise a practical question: PV glass that shifts the spectrum toward blue generates electricity while potentially enhancing circadian stimulation, but its effect on color rendering may make occupants uncomfortable with the visual quality of their environment.

Would a year-long field study in occupied offices — comparing alertness, sleep actigraphy, sick days, and cognitive performance in buildings with coordinated glazing-LED spectral systems versus standard fixed-spectrum installations — show that spectral coordination improves long-term occupant health beyond what either daylight or dynamic LED alone delivers?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0599.pdf

PDF-0600.title: Circadian lighting in architecture: Short-wavelength light, ipRGCs, and the design of time-sensitive environments
PDF-0600.text:
Light does more than illuminate surfaces — it sets the body's clock. This paper examines how exposure to light wavelengths, especially short-wavelength light at approximately 480 nanometers, drives circadian rhythm regulation via intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs), and what this photobiological mechanism means for architectural lighting design.

The circadian system is the body's master timekeeper. It synchronizes sleep, wakefulness, hormone secretion, body temperature, cognitive performance, and metabolic function to the 24-hour solar cycle. The primary time signal — the zeitgeber — is light entering the eye. But not all light is equally effective as a zeitgeber. The circadian system is maximally sensitive to short-wavelength light near 480 nm — the blue portion of the visible spectrum — because the ipRGCs that transmit light information to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (the brain's master clock) contain melanopsin, a photopigment with peak sensitivity at that wavelength.

This spectral sensitivity creates a design problem. The same short-wavelength light that the circadian system needs in the morning (to suppress melatonin, advance circadian phase, and promote alertness) is potentially disruptive in the evening (suppressing the melatonin onset that prepares the body for sleep). A building that provides appropriate circadian light must therefore vary its spectral output across the day: high short-wavelength content in the morning and midday to support circadian entrainment and alertness, transitioning to low short-wavelength content in the late afternoon and evening to protect melatonin onset and promote sleep readiness.

The architectural implications extend far beyond lamp selection. Building orientation determines when and how much daylight — which is naturally rich in short wavelengths — enters occupied spaces. East-facing windows provide morning daylight that supports circadian phase advance. West-facing windows provide afternoon daylight that, while thermally problematic, maintains circadian stimulation through the working day. North-facing windows (in the Northern Hemisphere) provide relatively stable, blue-rich skylight throughout the day. South-facing windows provide the most intense daylight but with variable short-wavelength content depending on cloud cover and solar angle.

Room depth determines how much circadian-effective light reaches occupants at interior positions. The circadian system requires light at the eye — vertical illuminance at corneal height — not light on the desk (horizontal illuminance at task height). A narrow room with tall windows delivers daylight deep into the space at eye level. A deep room with standard windows delivers daylight only to the perimeter zone; interior occupants receive primarily electric light with whatever spectral characteristics the luminaires provide.

Surface properties modulate the spectral content of reflected light. White surfaces reflect the full spectrum, preserving the short-wavelength content of daylight. Warm-colored surfaces (amber, terra cotta, beige) absorb short wavelengths and reflect longer wavelengths, shifting the reflected light toward the red end of the spectrum and reducing its melanopic efficacy. A room with white walls and ceiling redistributes circadian-effective light deep into the space; a room with warm-toned walls filters out the wavelengths that matter most for circadian health.

Electric lighting specification must align with the circadian requirement. Standard warm-white LEDs (2700K) have low melanopic content — suitable for evening environments where melatonin protection is the priority. Standard cool-white LEDs (4000–6500K) have higher melanopic content — suitable for morning and midday environments where alertness and circadian entrainment are the priority. Tunable LED systems that adjust spectral output across the day represent the ideal but are currently more expensive than fixed-spectrum systems.

The paper proposes a temporal architecture of light — specifying the lighting environment not as a static condition but as a dynamic, time-varying system that delivers the right spectral composition at the right time. Morning: high melanopic content through daylight admission and cool-spectrum electric lighting. Midday: sustained melanopic content through maintained daylight exposure. Afternoon: transitioning to reduced melanopic content as the body approaches its circadian evening. Evening: minimal melanopic content to protect melatonin onset.

For architects, the temporal dimension transforms lighting from a spatial design parameter to a spatiotemporal design parameter. The lighting that is correct at 8 AM is incorrect at 8 PM, and the building that cannot adjust its spectral delivery across the day is a building that is circadian-appropriate only part of the time. Operable windows (daylight access controlled by the occupant), automated shading (daylight admitted or blocked by time-of-day scheduling), and tunable electric lighting (spectral output adjusted by circadian algorithm) are the three systems that enable the temporal architecture of light.

The paper is theoretical, proposing design principles based on established photobiology without presenting new experimental data. The specific melanopic illuminance thresholds required for circadian entrainment at different times of day are not yet firmly established. Whether the architectural strategies proposed produce measurable circadian health benefits in occupied buildings has not been validated through field studies.

Would a field study — measuring melatonin onset timing, sleep actigraphy, alertness, and mood in office workers exposed to time-varying spectral lighting versus fixed-spectrum lighting over 12 weeks — provide the clinical evidence for temporal circadian lighting design?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0600.pdf

PDF-0602.title: Daylight-mimicking LED lighting preserves melatonin profiles while improving visual comfort
PDF-0602.text:
What if office lighting could look and feel like daylight without disrupting the hormones that regulate sleep? This study compared a daylight-mimicking LED system (dayLED) against a conventional LED system (conLED) in a controlled within-subjects experiment, measuring visual comfort, melatonin secretion, and sleep quality. The results suggest that spectrally tuned lighting can improve the subjective quality of the lit environment while preserving the circadian signals that conventional LED lighting threatens.

The dayLED condition was engineered to replicate the spectral power distribution of natural daylight — higher proportion of blue wavelengths in the morning to support circadian entrainment, transitioning to warmer spectrum in the afternoon. The conLED condition used standard commercial LED panels with a fixed correlated color temperature, the default specification in most new office buildings. Both conditions were matched for illuminance at the task plane; the difference was purely spectral — same photopic brightness, different spectral composition.

Subjects rated visual comfort under the dayLED condition as significantly better than during the conLED condition. The daylight-mimicking spectrum felt more natural, more pleasant, and more visually comfortable despite delivering the same number of lumens to the work surface. This finding confirms that visual comfort is not a simple function of illuminance — spectral composition independently affects how occupants perceive the quality of their lit environment. The dayLED spectrum's broader and smoother spectral distribution, which more closely matches the spectral content of natural daylight, produced a visual experience rated as superior to the narrow-band emission spectrum of conventional white LEDs.

The critical circadian question was whether the higher short-wavelength content of the dayLED system — which delivers more melanopic stimulation during morning hours — would disrupt melatonin secretion in the evening. The answer was no. A typical diurnal melatonin profile was observed in both conditions without significant difference between the dayLED and conLED conditions. The daylight-mimicking spectrum's morning blue boost did not suppress or delay the evening melatonin rise. This null finding is physiologically important: it demonstrates that the circadian system can receive appropriate morning blue light stimulation (supporting alertness, mood, and sleep-wake cycle entrainment) without paying a melatonin penalty in the evening, provided the spectrum shifts appropriately across the day.

However, the melatonin data included a suggestive nuance. Evening melatonin values prior to the treatment night showed a pattern that visual inspection and post hoc comparison suggested might reflect lower melatonin values in one condition. While this difference did not reach statistical significance in the primary analysis, it raises the possibility that the spectral conditions may produce subtle circadian effects that a larger sample or longer exposure period might detect. This is a methodological caution, not a positive finding — but it indicates that the "no difference" conclusion should be held provisionally until confirmed by larger studies.

For architects and lighting designers, the study provides evidence that the spectrum of office lighting is a health-relevant design variable, not merely a color rendering specification. Current lighting practice specifies color rendering index (CRI) and correlated color temperature (CCT) as the primary spectral quality measures. But CRI and CCT do not capture the melanopic effectiveness of the spectrum — two light sources with identical CRI and CCT can deliver very different melanopic stimulation depending on their spectral power distribution in the 460–490 nm range. The dayLED system demonstrates that spectral engineering can achieve high visual comfort and appropriate circadian stimulation simultaneously — but only if the lighting specification includes melanopic content as a design parameter alongside CRI and CCT.

The practical design response is spectral prescription by time of day. Morning lighting should deliver high melanopic content (elevated short-wavelength power) to support circadian phase advance, alertness, and mood. Afternoon lighting should begin transitioning to reduced melanopic content. Evening lighting — for occupants who work past 5 PM — should deliver minimal melanopic content to protect melatonin onset. The dayLED system achieved this by dynamically tuning its spectral output across the day, but a simpler implementation could use two or three preset spectral programs triggered by time-of-day scheduling.

The study tested a small sample over a limited exposure period. Whether the benefits of daylight-mimicking LED lighting accumulate over weeks and months of continuous exposure — or whether occupants habituate to the spectral difference — is an important clinical question for long-term workplace health. The study was conducted in a controlled laboratory setting; field deployment in occupied offices may produce different results due to varying daylight availability, occupant control behavior, and the confounding effects of computer screens.

Would a year-long field study — deploying daylight-mimicking LED systems in half the floors of a commercial office building while maintaining conventional LED on the other half, measuring sleep actigraphy, melatonin (from saliva), alertness, visual comfort, and sick leave quarterly — demonstrate a sustained health and comfort advantage for spectrally tuned office lighting?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0602.pdf

PDF-0607.title: PDF-0607
PDF-0607.text:
Background speech disrupts working memory more than aircraft noise does — and the two noise types activate different brain networks. Bjørn Sætrevik and Patrik Sörqvist used fMRI in this 2015 study in the *Scandinavian Journal of Psychology* to show that updating working memory in the presence of aircraft noise and speech noise engages dissociable neural mechanisms, suggesting that the reasons why background speech is more detrimental involve qualitatively different cognitive interference, not merely louder or more variable sound.

Participants performed a number-updating task — a working memory paradigm that distinguishes between "substitution processes" (adding new items and suppressing old items to maintain the current memory set) and "exclusion processes" (rejecting newly presented items to keep the existing memory set intact). The task was performed under three background conditions: silence, aircraft noise, and background speech.

The key fMRI finding: substitution and exclusion processes activated different brain networks, and these activations were modulated differently by noise type. Substitution processes activated dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), posterior medial frontal cortex, and parietal lobes. Exclusion processes activated anterior medial frontal cortex. Critically, the prefrontal cortex was activated more by substitution processes during *speech noise* than during *aircraft noise* — suggesting that background speech places additional demands on the executive control mechanisms that manage working memory updating.

This dissociation supports the hypothesis that background speech is more detrimental than aircraft noise because speech is automatically processed by the language comprehension system, creating semantic interference that competes with the working memory task. Aircraft noise, while loud and unpleasant, does not engage the same language processing machinery and therefore imposes a different — and apparently lesser — burden on working memory updating.

For architectural acoustics and workplace design, this finding has direct implications: the semantic content of background noise matters more than its physical intensity. An open-plan office with intelligible background speech may impair cognitive performance more than one near an airport runway — not because the speech is louder, but because it hijacks the neural machinery needed for the very cognitive operations people are trying to perform. Acoustic design should prioritize speech privacy and intelligible-speech masking over overall noise level reduction.

What parametric study — varying both noise type (speech, speech-shaped noise, aircraft noise) and noise level (equated dB vs. typical field levels) while measuring both behavioural working memory performance and fMRI activation in prefrontal and parietal networks — could disentangle the contributions of semantic content and acoustic intensity to working memory disruption?

PDF-0613.title: Classroom noise impairs cognitive performance: Background sound effects on children's learning
PDF-0613.text:
Children learn worse when classrooms are loud — and most classrooms are loud. This study tested the effect of classroom noise versus silence on cognitive performance, finding a significant main effect of background sound condition on learning outcomes. The result quantifies what teachers have always known and what acoustic engineers have long warned about: background noise in educational spaces directly impairs the cognitive processes that learning depends on.

The experimental design compared two conditions: classroom noise (recorded real-world classroom sound at a representative level, including speech babble, movement noise, furniture scraping, and HVAC hum) and silence (a quiet condition with minimal background sound). Children performed cognitive tasks — memory, attention, reading comprehension, or mathematical reasoning — under both conditions, and performance was compared.

The significant main effect means that classroom noise impaired performance relative to silence across the sample. Children remembered less, attended less accurately, comprehended less, or solved fewer problems when the background sound environment contained typical classroom noise. The impairment is not subtle — noise-induced performance decrements in educational settings typically range from 5 to 25 percent depending on the task type, noise level, and age of the child.

The mechanism is primarily attentional. The auditory system processes all incoming sound, including irrelevant background noise, and the central executive must suppress this processing to maintain focus on the learning task. This suppression consumes cognitive resources that would otherwise be available for learning. For adults with mature executive function, the resource cost of noise suppression is manageable — annoying but not debilitating. For children, whose executive function is still developing, the cost is proportionally larger. A seven-year-old devotes a larger fraction of their total attentional capacity to suppressing classroom noise than a thirty-year-old adult would, leaving less capacity available for the learning task.

Speech noise is particularly damaging because the auditory system is obligatorily attuned to intelligible speech. When a child can hear fragments of conversation from adjacent groups — even unintentionally — the language processing system engages automatically, diverting attentional resources from the learning task to the overheard speech. This is the irrelevant speech effect, one of the most robust findings in auditory cognition. Any intelligible speech in the background impairs serial recall, reading comprehension, and complex reasoning — not because the speech is loud but because it is meaningful.

The acoustic conditions in typical classrooms are far from optimal. Reverberation times in many classrooms exceed 0.6 seconds — well above the 0.4-second target recommended by acoustic guidelines for primary school classrooms. High reverberation reduces speech clarity (the teacher's voice is smeared by reflections), increases the overall noise level (sound energy persists longer in a reverberant room), and degrades the signal-to-noise ratio at the student's position. Students in reverberant classrooms must work harder to understand the teacher and harder to suppress the ambient noise — a double cognitive penalty.

Background noise from HVAC systems adds a continuous noise floor that further degrades the learning environment. Many classroom HVAC systems produce 40–50 dB(A) of background noise — enough to mask soft speech at distance and to require the teacher to raise their voice to be heard, which in turn raises the overall noise level and increases vocal strain.

For architects designing schools, the study prescribes three acoustic priorities. First, reduce reverberation: specify high-absorption ceiling tiles (NRC ≥ 0.85) and add absorptive wall panels, particularly at the rear wall where reflected energy accumulates. A RT₆₀ of 0.4 seconds or less in primary classrooms and 0.5 seconds or less in secondary classrooms is the design target. Second, reduce HVAC noise: specify mechanical systems that achieve NC-25 or lower in teaching spaces. This typically requires oversized ductwork (lower air velocity), acoustically lined ducts, and vibration-isolated equipment. Third, reduce speech transmission between groups: in open-plan or flexible learning environments, use acoustic partitions, sound masking, and spatial separation to maintain speech privacy between learning groups.

The economic argument is direct. Acoustic treatment is among the least expensive building improvements and among the most impactful on educational outcomes. A classroom retrofit of ceiling tiles and wall panels may cost $2,000–5,000 and can reduce reverberation time by 0.2–0.4 seconds, producing a measurable improvement in speech intelligibility and a corresponding improvement in learning outcomes.

The study tested acute effects of noise on performance in controlled conditions. Whether chronic noise exposure — years of learning in acoustically poor classrooms — produces cumulative academic deficits beyond the acute performance impairment is not tested but plausible. Individual differences in noise sensitivity, hearing ability, and language proficiency may moderate the effect.

Would a longitudinal study — measuring academic achievement over two school years in classrooms before and after acoustic renovation (reverberation reduction, HVAC quieting, speech privacy improvement) — quantify the cumulative impact of classroom acoustic quality on learning outcomes?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0613.pdf

PDF-0617.title: PDF-0617
PDF-0617.text:
Does walking through a building teach you the layout better than being shown a map? Elizabeth Chrastil and William Warren at Brown University reviewed the evidence in this 2012 theoretical review in Psychonomic Bulletin and Review. The answer is yes — but only for certain kinds of spatial knowledge.

The distinction between active and passive spatial learning matters for anyone who designs buildings, campuses, or cities. If active exploration builds better mental maps, then buildings should be designed to encourage walking and exploration rather than relying on signage and directories.

Chrastil and Warren identified five components of active spatial learning: idiothetic information from self-movement (vestibular and proprioceptive), decision-making at choice points, attention to place-action links, encoding of spatial relations, and mental manipulation of spatial knowledge. Walking contributes metric survey knowledge — the kind needed for shortcuts and detours. Decision-making contributes route knowledge. Landmarks and boundaries are picked up with or without active movement. Route and survey knowledge rely on different memory systems and are stored independently in working memory.

For architects and wayfinding designers, the review supports: design buildings that encourage exploratory walking (loops, visible destinations, choice points) rather than one-path corridors. Give people reasons to move through the building, and their mental maps will improve.

The review is theoretical. Most evidence comes from desktop VR, which lacks vestibular input.

Would a study comparing wayfinding accuracy in a real hospital — one group guided by a digital map, one group encouraged to walk freely for an equal period — show that active exploration produces faster, more accurate navigation in subsequent visits? A hospital wayfinding study would test the clinical design value of active exploration.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0617.pdf

PDF-0618.title: PDF-0618: Brozzoli et al. (2013) — A Shared Representation of the Space Near Oneself and Others in the Human Premotor Cortex
PDF-0618.text:
# PDF-0618: Brozzoli et al. (2013) — A Shared Representation of the Space Near Oneself and Others in the Human Premotor Cortex

## Paper Type
Empirical research (within-subjects fMRI, 2 experiments; *Current Biology* Report)

## Core Finding
The human ventral premotor cortex (PMv) contains neuronal populations that encode the space near both one's own hand and another person's hand using the same body-centered coordinate system. This "shared peripersonal space" representation—analogous to mirror neurons for actions—could support social interaction by allowing the brain to automatically process the spatial environment around other people as if it were one's own.

## Methods & Sample
- **Experiment 1**: N = 26 (19–35 years, mean 28 ± 5; 18 males), fMRI BOLD adaptation paradigm
- **Experiment 2**: N = 20 (22–42 years, mean 29 ± 6; 12 males), bidirectional adaptation
- **Scanner**: Siemens TIM Trio 3T, 12-channel head coil, gradient echo T2*-weighted EPI (40 contiguous 3mm slices, 0.1mm gap, FOV = 58 × 76 matrix, 3 × 3mm in-plane, TE = 40ms, TR = 2,540ms)

**Stimulus Setup** (Figure 1):
- Participant supine in MRI, head tilted 30° forward, right hand on table (42 × 35cm), another person's right hand visible on same table at 100cm distance
- Visual stimulus: small red ball (3cm diameter) on a wooden stick (50cm) moved up and down 2cm above index finger for 3s periods, by trained experimenter hidden behind curtains
- Control: prosthetic 3D rubber hand at same locations
- Factorial design: prosthetic hand position × other's hand position × stimulus location

## Key Statistics
**Experiment 1** (n = 26):
- Left inferior precentral sulcus (PMv): **T = 3.95, p_FWE-corrected = 0.015**, MNI [-46, 2, 14]
- Shows shared representation: greater BOLD adaptation to stimulation near both participant's AND other's hand vs. near prosthetic hand

**Experiment 2** (n = 20):
- Left PMv: **T = 3.77, p_FWE-corrected = 0.016**, MNI [-42, 2, 24] — bidirectional adaptation confirms shared encoding regardless of stimulus presentation order
- Right anterior cingulate cortex (ACC): **T = 3.73, p_FWE-corrected = 0.047** — shows greater selectivity for space near the other person's hand, suggesting ACC may support "mentalizing" the other's space

## Visual Content
**Figure 1** (page 2): Four-panel experimental setup showing (A) participant's view with own hand and other's hand on table, (B) temporal schema of stimulation trials (3s near self → 3s near other, with 7 ± 4s ITI), (C) spatial arrangement (30cm between hands, 100cm viewing distance), (D) full factorial prosthetic × other hand × stimulus location matrix.

**Figure 2** (page 3): Brain activation maps and bar plots. (A) Exp. 1 — left PMv cluster on inflated brain, with adaptation index bars showing Self-to-Other and Other-to-Self adaptation equally strong for self and other (Left PMv [-46,2,14], T=3.95, p_FWE=0.015). (B) Exp. 2 — left PMv [-42,2,24], T=3.77, p_FWE=0.016.

**Figure 3** (page 4): Experiment 2 methods. (A) Spatial arrangement with "Far" (100cm) and "Near Self" positions. (B) Factorial stimulus matrix. 

**Table 1** (page 3): MNI coordinates, peak T-values, and p-values for Experiment 1 (left inferior precentral/sulcus PMv: T=3.95, p=0.015; right putamen: T=3.15, p=0.042; right anterior cingulate: T=3.66, p=0.001 uncorrected).

**Table 2** (page 4): Experiment 2 results — left inferior precentral: T=3.77, p=0.016; left inferior temporal gyrus: T=5.13, p=0.036.

## Mechanism & Theory
The findings extend the mirror neuron framework from action coding to spatial coding. Just as mirror neurons in monkey F5/PMv fire both when performing and observing actions, the human PMv encodes peripersonal space using body-centered coordinates that generalize across self and other. This shared spatial representation may support joint attention, action prediction, and cooperative behavior by providing a common spatial reference frame.

## Design Implications
The discovery that the brain automatically maps the space near other people using one's own body-centered coordinates has implications for architectural design of collaborative and social spaces. The ~100cm distance at which the "other's space" was encoded corresponds to the near edge of Hall's social distance zone, suggesting that architectural layouts placing people within this range (e.g., shared desks, collaborative workstations) may activate these shared spatial representations, potentially facilitating cooperation.

## Key References
- Rizzolatti et al. (1981, 1988, 1997): Peripersonal neurons in macaque PMv
- Brozzoli et al. (2011, 2012): fMRI adaptation for near-hand space coding
- Caggiano et al. (2009): Mirror neurons encode peripersonal/extrapersonal space
- Heed et al. (2010): Others reduce crossmodal integration in peripersonal space

PDF-0619.title: PDF-0619
PDF-0619.text:
Buildings are becoming computational. They sense occupants through cameras, microphones, and environmental sensors. They respond through adaptive lighting, HVAC, motorized facades, and interactive displays. But the field of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) has largely ignored the building as a computing platform, and architecture has largely ignored the building as an interactive system. Hamed Alavi, Elizabeth F. Churchill, Mikael Wiberg, Denis Lalanne, and colleagues bridge this gap in this 2019 paper in *ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction* by defining "Human-Building Interaction" (HBI) as a new research area at the intersection of HCI, architecture, and urban design.

The paper argues that buildings are the largest, most permanent, and most consequential interactive systems humans encounter. A smartphone is used for hours; a building is inhabited for decades. Yet the design principles for building interactivity are borrowed haphazardly from interface design traditions that were developed for screens, not spaces.

The authors identify three types of interaction between humans and buildings. *Implicit interaction*: the building senses and responds to occupant behaviour without deliberate action — adaptive lighting that adjusts to occupancy, ventilation that responds to CO₂ levels, facades that track the sun. *Explicit interaction*: occupants consciously interact with building systems — adjusting thermostats, activating blinds, using interactive wayfinding displays. *Ambient interaction*: the building communicates information to occupants through environmental cues — colour-shifting walls that indicate air quality, floor surfaces that provide wayfinding through texture changes, soundscapes that adapt to social activity.

The paper proposes research challenges for HBI: (1) *temporality* — buildings operate on timescales from seconds (lighting adjustment) to decades (renovation), requiring interaction design that accommodates this range; (2) *scale* — buildings are inhabited by many people simultaneously, creating multi-user interaction design challenges that do not arise with personal devices; (3) *embodiment* — building interaction is spatial, bodily, and multisensory, not screen-mediated; (4) *agency* — buildings increasingly have autonomous capabilities (smart HVAC, predictive maintenance), raising questions about the appropriate distribution of control between human and machine.

For neuroarchitecture and environmental psychology, the HBI framework provides the computational infrastructure for implementing and testing evidence-based design principles in real time. If ceiling height affects mood, a building with HBI capabilities can adjust the perceived spatial volume through movable ceilings, adaptive lighting that enhances or diminishes perceived height, or projected visual boundaries — and monitor the occupant response through embedded physiological sensors.

What in-situ experiment — deploying HBI systems that adapt lighting colour temperature, spatial openness, and soundscapes in real-time based on occupant physiological state (wearable HRV, cortisol) — could demonstrate closed-loop environmental optimization for occupant wellbeing?

PDF-0621.title: PDF-0621
PDF-0621.text:
Walk into an unfamiliar building and within seconds you know which corner feels most open, which hallway leads somewhere worth exploring, and which alcove feels sheltered. You measured nothing. You read no floor plan. Your eyes computed these spatial qualities from the geometry of visible space around you. Gerald Franz and Jan Wiener showed in this 2008 paper in Environment and Planning B that isovist analysis can capture exactly these qualities in numbers that predict how people feel about a space and how they move through it.

Architects have long described spaces as cramped, open, inviting, or confusing. These words carry real weight. Agoraphobia and claustrophobia show that spatial size alone can trigger intense fear. Hall's proxemics (1966) showed that people weight nearby space more heavily than distant space. Kaplan's framework (1988) proposed that people prefer spaces that balance order with mystery. Appleton's prospect-refuge theory (1988) argued that humans evolved to seek places that offer both wide views and nearby cover. But none of these theories came with numbers that a computer could use. Space syntax offered graphs and metrics, but nobody had tested whether those metrics predict what people actually feel.

Franz and Wiener bridged this gap. They derived isovist measures from each theory: isovist area captures how much space you can see. Free near space captures how open the area right around you feels. Jaggedness, vertex count, and polygon sections capture how complex the boundary looks. Revelation captures how much new space opens up as you move forward, serving as a formal proxy for the Kaplans' mystery.

In their first experiment, people rated sixteen virtual indoor scenes on four scales: how pleasant, how beautiful, how interesting, and how spacious each scene felt. Six global isovist measures predicted all four ratings with striking power. Isovist area and free near space predicted spaciousness ratings (R-squared = 0.78). Vertex count and roundness predicted beauty ratings (R-squared = 0.78). Density and symmetry axes predicted how arousing people found the scenes (R-squared = 0.73). Overall, these six numbers explained 69 to 78 percent of the variance in how people judged each scene.

In the second experiment, sixteen people navigated the same scenes in search of the best hiding place and the best lookout spot. Franz and Wiener tracked their paths: how long they took, how far they walked, how sharply they turned, and how often they stopped. Jaggedness, clustering, openness, and revelation all correlated with wayfinding speed at r-squared above 0.35 (p < 0.02). Neighborhood size and vertex count did not predict how people navigated.

Figure 4 (p. 581) shows bar charts of these results side by side. Some measures, like jaggedness and clustering, predict both how people feel about a space and how they move through it. Others, like neighborhood size, predict neither. This split matters: it means that some spatial features act as genuine perceptual cues that guide both affect and behavior, while others are merely geometric artifacts.

Figure 7 (p. 585) shows six floor plans with spatial centers detected by an algorithm based on local maxima of wall distance. The algorithm picks the spots that human observers independently chose as the center of each room, supporting the claim that isovists capture real perceptual qualities of space.

For architects who use computational design tools, the method is direct: generate isovist maps during schematic design and tune the six measures to match the desired feel. A hospital waiting room should maximize openness and free near space to feel spacious and pleasant. A meditation room should minimize jaggedness to feel calm. A museum should maximize revelation to create a sense of mystery that draws visitors forward.

The study tested sixteen scenes with sixteen participants. That is a small stimulus set for a regression with six predictors, and a small sample for generalizing to broad human responses. No physiological data were collected. The virtual rooms had no furniture, no other people, no lighting contrasts, and no sound. Real buildings have all of these, and isovists capture none of them. The strong R-squared values in this stripped-down setting may shrink when real-world confounds enter the picture.

Can optimizing isovist profiles in real hospital corridors reduce how long patients take to find their destination and how anxious they feel while searching? A controlled trial comparing wayfinding speed and state anxiety in two wings, one designed with isovist-tuned spatial profiles and one designed by standard practice, would test whether these geometric numbers translate to measurable benefit in real buildings.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0621.pdf
