PDF-0819.title: Building Wellbeing: The SACRA model for quantifying psychological outcomes of architectural design
PDF-0819.text:
Can you measure what a building does to your mind? This review proposes that you can — and that you must. The Building Wellbeing scale, based on the SACRA model, provides a multi-item measurement instrument that produces quantified and transferable psychological wellbeing metrics for architectural evaluation. The ambition is to make psychological wellbeing a specifiable, measurable, and verifiable building performance criterion — as concrete and actionable as energy use, structural load, or acoustic isolation.

The SACRA model identifies five dimensions of building-related psychological wellbeing. Safety: the perception that the building protects occupants from physical harm, environmental hazard, and social threat — a precondition for all other wellbeing outcomes because psychological engagement with a space requires a baseline sense of physical security. Aesthetics: the perceptual and emotional response to the building's formal, material, and spatial qualities — the dimension that conventional architectural culture already attends to, but that evidence-based design has struggled to operationalize. Comfort: the physiological satisfaction of thermal, acoustic, luminous, and air quality conditions — the dimension that building science addresses through environmental engineering but that subjective perception moderates substantially. Relatedness: the social quality of the environment — whether the building supports connection, communication, community, and social identity or produces isolation, anonymity, and social fragmentation. Autonomy: the degree of personal control the building provides over environmental conditions and spatial use — the perception of agency that research consistently identifies as the strongest single predictor of occupant satisfaction.

The five SACRA dimensions correspond to established psychological constructs from self-determination theory (autonomy and relatedness), environmental psychology (safety and comfort), and neuroaesthetics (aesthetics). The model's innovation is not the identification of these dimensions — each has been studied independently for decades — but their integration into a single measurement framework that can be applied to any building type and that produces scores comparable across buildings, time points, and populations.

The Building Wellbeing scale operationalizes each dimension through multiple questionnaire items. Safety is assessed through items measuring perceived physical security, environmental hazard awareness, and social security in the building. Aesthetics is assessed through items measuring visual pleasure, spatial engagement, and material appreciation. Comfort is assessed through items measuring thermal, acoustic, visual, and air quality satisfaction. Relatedness is assessed through items measuring social interaction opportunity, community feeling, and social belonging in the building. Autonomy is assessed through items measuring perceived control over temperature, lighting, ventilation, noise exposure, and spatial arrangement.

The scale produces numerical scores on each dimension that can be aggregated into an overall Building Wellbeing index. This index is the paper's most practically important contribution: it provides a single number that represents the psychological performance of a building, analogous to the energy use intensity (EUI) that represents its energy performance or the weighted sound reduction index (Rw) that represents its acoustic performance. A building with a high Building Wellbeing score produces measurably better psychological outcomes for its occupants than a building with a low score — and the score can be specified in the design brief, predicted during design development, and verified through post-occupancy evaluation.

For architects, the SACRA framework translates vague aspirations ("we want a building that makes people feel good") into specific design targets on five measurable dimensions. A design team can set target scores for each SACRA dimension during programming, evaluate design alternatives against these targets during design development, and verify achievement during post-occupancy evaluation. If the Comfort score is below target, the building science team adjusts environmental systems. If the Autonomy score is below target, personal controls are added. If the Relatedness score is below target, social spaces are redesigned. If the Aesthetics score is below target, material and spatial qualities are reconsidered. Each dimension maps to specific design decisions that can be modified to improve the score.

The broader implication is accountability. If psychological wellbeing is measurable, it is also contractable — design teams can commit to achieving minimum wellbeing scores, and commissioning processes can verify whether those commitments are met. This shifts occupant wellbeing from an unverifiable design intention to a performance specification with the same contractual standing as energy performance, fire safety, and structural adequacy.

The scale is proposed as a research instrument, not yet validated as a building performance standard. Whether the five SACRA dimensions are exhaustive, whether they are equally weighted, and whether they interact (does high autonomy compensate for low comfort?) are empirical questions requiring large-sample validation studies. The cultural generalizability of the instrument — whether the same five dimensions matter equally across cultures with different values, expectations, and architectural traditions — is acknowledged as a limitation.

Would a validation study — deploying the Building Wellbeing scale in 50 buildings across five building types and ten countries, then correlating scores with objective health outcomes (sick leave, cortisol, sleep quality) and satisfaction measures — establish the instrument's predictive validity and cross-cultural reliability?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0819.pdf

PDF-0820.title: PDF-0820: Neuroscience Forefront Review on neural mechanisms of material perception, termed shitsukan in Japan
PDF-0820.text:
# PDF-0820: Neuroscience Forefront Review on neural mechanisms of material perception, termed shitsukan in Japan

## Paper Type
narrative_review

## Core Argument
Neuroscience Forefront Review on neural mechanisms of material perception, termed shitsukan in Japanese (quality-sense). Material perception entails perceiving and recognizing surface quality or internal state of an object based on sensory stimuli. Cortical areas in the ventral visual pathway are strongly involved. Vision is the main focus but every sensory modality contributes. Information obtained through different sensory modalities is closely linked in material perception — cross-modal processing is an important feature. Reviews advances in image statistics approaches, fMRI neuro-imaging, and psychophysics of material perception

## Paper Type
empirical_research (None)

## Core Finding
Carnegie Mellon University Masters thesis (2022) analyzing the impact of material, formal, and aesthetic architectural features on stress reactions using biophilic patterns. Uses architectural psychology framework to examine how specific design elements — materials, spatial forms, and aesthetic qualities — influence occupant stress through biophilic design patterns. Investigates the relationship between biophilic design interventions and measurable stress reduction in architectural contexts

Analyzing the Impact of Material, Formal, and Aesthetic Features on Stress Reactions

PDF-0822.title: Haptic feedback in VR enhances material and temperature perception — making virtual surfaces feel real
PDF-0822.text:
Virtual reality can show you a marble countertop, but can it make you feel its coolness and smoothness? Steeven Villa and colleagues examined whether ultrasound mid-air haptic feedback enhances material and temperature perception in virtual environments, bridging the gap between visual simulation and multisensory architectural experience.

The study tested whether adding haptic cues — delivered by an ultrasound-based mid-air haptic device that creates tactile patterns on the user's skin without physical contact — improved participants' ability to identify materials and perceive temperature in a VR environment. Participants interacted with virtual surfaces (wood, stone, metal, fabric) rendered with varying visual textures and temperatures. In some conditions, haptic feedback patterns were designed to correspond to the expected tactile properties of each material (smooth and cool for metal, warm and rough for wood). In control conditions, no haptic feedback was provided, or incongruent haptic feedback was delivered.

The results showed that congruent haptic feedback significantly enhanced participants' material identification accuracy and temperature perception compared to vision-only or incongruent conditions. When the haptic pattern matched the expected feel of the virtual material, participants were faster and more confident in their assessments. The multisensory integration produced a more convincing perceptual experience than either modality alone.

For architects using VR as a design and client communication tool, the finding addresses a key limitation: standard VR presents architecture as a purely visual experience, missing the tactile dimension that profoundly shapes how built spaces are perceived. Walking into a stone church, touching a wooden handrail, feeling the temperature difference between a sunlit terrace and a shaded corridor — these tactile experiences contribute to architectural meaning in ways that visual rendering alone cannot capture.

Integrating haptic feedback into architectural VR allows designers and clients to evaluate material choices through multisensory experience before construction. The cool smoothness of polished concrete versus the warm grain of oak can be felt, not just seen — potentially changing material selection decisions that are currently made on visual appearance alone.

The haptic technology is still limited: ultrasound mid-air devices produce gentle tactile patterns on exposed skin but cannot simulate the full range of tactile properties (weight, rigidity, fine texture) that real materials provide. The resolution and intensity of mid-air haptics are lower than contact-based haptic devices. The study used controlled stimuli that are simpler than the complex material surfaces of real architecture.

What user study — having architects evaluate the same building design in three VR modalities (visual only, visual + congruent haptics, visual + incongruent haptics) and measuring material preference decisions, client satisfaction, and design revision requests — could test whether haptic-enhanced VR changes architectural design decisions compared to standard visual-only VR?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0822.pdf

PDF-0823.title: Material selection in architecture shapes aesthetic expectations — and the two interact in ways designers rarely test
PDF-0823.text:
The material a building is made from — or appears to be made from — shapes the aesthetic expectations visitors bring to it before they experience anything else. This 2010 study in *METU JFA* investigates the relationship between material selection and aesthetic expectation in architecture, arguing that material perception is not a passive registration of surface properties but an active, expectation-laden interpretation shaped by prior experience, cultural associations, and sensory memory.

Every material carries associations. Wood signals warmth, craft, and natural origin. Glass signals modernity, transparency, and precision. Concrete signals mass, permanence, and industrial process. Stone signals geological time and cultural tradition. These associations are not universal — they vary with culture, experience, and context — but they are real psychological phenomena that operate before and during the aesthetic evaluation of a building.

The review synthesizes evidence from material science, perceptual psychology, and architectural theory to identify the variables that mediate the material-aesthetics relationship. Surface texture is primary: the human visual system is tuned to detect surface texture at close range, using texture gradients to estimate material properties (hardness, roughness, warmth, age). Materials that offer rich texture information — natural stone, weathered wood, exposed aggregate concrete — engage the visual and haptic systems more fully than materials that suppress texture — polished steel, sealed glass, painted gypsum.

Temporal behavior matters: materials that change visibly over time (patina, staining, biological colonization) create a narrative of use and aging that contributes to aesthetic meaning. Materials that resist change (stainless steel, anodized aluminum, sealed surfaces) maintain their original appearance but do not accumulate the temporal character that many observers find aesthetically compelling. The choice between a material that weathers and one that resists is an aesthetic decision with consequences that unfold over the building's lifespan.

For architects, the review frames material specification as an aesthetic design decision that operates at the perceptual and associative level, not just the structural or cost level. The material palette of a building establishes the sensory vocabulary through which occupants and visitors interpret the space — warmth or coolness, permanence or transience, craft or industry, nature or technology. These interpretations shape emotional responses that persist through repeated use.

The review is analytical and theoretical, drawing on established literature rather than reporting new experiments. The claims about material associations are grounded in prior research but are not tested in the specific architectural contexts discussed. Cultural variation in material aesthetics is acknowledged in principle but not systematically investigated.

What cross-cultural experiment — presenting the same architectural designs rendered in different material palettes (wood, concrete, glass, stone) to participants from different cultural backgrounds while measuring aesthetic preference, emotional association, and haptic curiosity — could map how material-aesthetic associations vary across cultures?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0823.pdf

PDF-0824.title: Haptic design from blind users: Experience-based parameters for inclusive architectural practice
PDF-0824.text:
The people who know most about how buildings feel are the people who cannot see them. This qualitative study drew on experience-based haptic design parameters from blind users to produce more inclusive architectural design — reversing the usual direction of expertise by positioning visually impaired individuals not as users requiring accommodation but as expert informants whose embodied knowledge of tactile spatial experience exceeds that of any sighted designer.

The methodological innovation is important. Rather than asking sighted architects to imagine what blind users need, the study asked blind users to describe the tactile qualities of their built environments — what they notice, what helps them, what confuses them, what feels good, and what feels dangerous. The resulting haptic design parameters emerge from lived experience rather than theoretical speculation, grounding the design recommendations in the phenomenology of tactile spatial navigation.

The first set of parameters concerns surface differentiation. Blind users identify spatial zones, thresholds, and decision points primarily through changes in floor surface texture and material. A transition from carpet to tile indicates a threshold between a private room and a public corridor. From smooth concrete to rough stone indicates a transition from interior to exterior. From resilient flooring to hard flooring indicates a transition from a rest zone to a circulation zone. These tactile transitions are perceived through the soles of the feet and through the vibration transmitted through shoes and walking aids. The design implication is specific: every significant spatial boundary should be marked by a tactile material change detectible underfoot.

The second set concerns handrail and wall texture. Blind users trail walls and follow handrails as continuous navigation guides. Changes in handrail profile (round to flat, smooth to grooved) signal changes in spatial condition (approaching a landing, reaching a floor, arriving at a door). Changes in wall texture (smooth plaster to rough brick, flat paint to textured wallpaper) provide orientation cues that supplement floor information. The design implication: handrails should be continuous, graspable, and tactilely coded at key locations; walls should vary in texture at decision points.

The third set concerns thermal and air movement cues. Blind users report using radiant heat from windows (warm patches on the skin indicating a glazed wall), air movement from openings (drafts indicating doors, corridors, or ventilation grilles), and temperature gradients between zones (cooler near exterior walls, warmer near interior cores) as spatial orientation aids. These cues are available to sighted users as well but are largely ignored because visual information makes them redundant. For blind users, they are primary navigation data. The design implication: allow thermal variation within buildings rather than suppressing it with uniform mechanical conditioning, because that variation carries spatial information.

The fourth set concerns acoustic spatial markers. While not strictly haptic, acoustic cues are inseparable from the blind user's spatial experience and were consistently reported alongside tactile parameters. Different ceiling heights produce different reverberation signatures. Door openings create acoustic portals (the sound field changes when passing from a corridor into a room). Water features provide continuous orientation anchors (the fountain sound marks the center of the atrium). Hard floors echo footsteps, providing self-generated echo-location information. The design implication: manage acoustic character as a spatial orientation tool, not just a comfort parameter.

The inclusive design argument extends beyond the blind population. The haptic design parameters identified by blind users describe environmental qualities that benefit all users in conditions of reduced visual information: dim lighting, unfamiliar buildings, emergency evacuations, cognitive overload, distraction, and age-related visual decline. A building designed with rich tactile differentiation, continuous haptic guidance, thermal spatial cues, and acoustic spatial markers is navigable by touch alone — and this navigability provides a safety margin for every occupant, not just those with permanent visual impairment.

For architects, the study's most powerful insight is that tactile spatial quality is currently invisible in design practice. Plans, sections, and renderings communicate nothing about how a building feels to the hand, the foot, and the skin. Material specifications typically describe visual appearance and structural performance but not haptic properties (roughness, warmth, grip, yield, elasticity). The gap between what the eye sees and what the body touches is a design gap that inclusive architecture must close.

The study is qualitative with a small sample of blind and visually impaired participants. Whether the haptic parameters identified generalize across different types of visual impairment (congenital versus acquired, partial versus total), different cultural contexts, and different building types requires larger-scale investigation.

Would a comparative wayfinding study — testing navigation accuracy, speed, and confidence in buildings designed with versus without the blind-user-derived haptic design parameters, using both blind and sighted-blindfolded participants — quantify the functional benefit of experience-based inclusive design?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0824.pdf

PDF-0825.title: Building material perception as a design tool for upgrading architecture
PDF-0825.text:
Run your hand along a ceramic tile that has been printed with a wood-grain texture. Your eyes register warmth — the golden tones, the organic grain — but your fingertips report something cold, hard, and unyielding. The mismatch jars. That perceptual conflict sits at the center of this paper, which argues that the gap between how a building material looks and how it feels, sounds, and smells is not a flaw to be hidden but a design variable to be deliberately controlled.

The paper builds a theoretical framework for using material perception as a compositional tool in architecture. Its central claim is that architects already manipulate material perception — every painted concrete surface that mimics stone, every laminate floor that imitates hardwood, every glass balustrade over a thirty-story drop — but they do so without a systematic understanding of the perceptual mechanisms involved. The framework aims to make that implicit practice explicit and evidence-informed. This is a theoretical contribution that synthesizes perception research, material science, and architectural case studies rather than reporting new empirical data.

The authors organize their argument around two themes. The first addresses the theoretical foundations of material perception in architecture. Perception operates at multiple levels: a sensory level (reception of visual, tactile, auditory, and olfactory information), a cognitive level (interpretation and categorization of that information), and an evaluative level (emotional and aesthetic responses). Building materials engage all three. The first impression a person forms when viewing a material surface — typically within thirty seconds — draws primarily on vision, but touch follows rapidly and can either confirm or contradict the visual signal. When a fabricated material carries the texture of a natural one (ceramic printed with wood grain, composite panels molded with stone relief), the receiver's impression shifts: the visual form registers one material category while the tactile feedback registers another. The paper argues that this crossed signal can produce a sense of deception and discomfort, or — when handled deliberately — a sense of surprise, sophistication, or conceptual playfulness.

The second theme addresses material classification and transformation techniques. The paper distinguishes between natural materials (wood, stone, brick, metal) and fabricated or composite materials (polymers, engineered surfaces, digitally printed substrates). It catalogs techniques by which materials gain new perceptual identities: surface texturing (embossing, etching, casting), coating and layering (paint, veneer, anodization), transparency manipulation (frosting, tinting, laminating), and digital printing (photorealistic reproduction of absent materials). Each technique shifts the perceptual signature of the base material along one or more sensory dimensions while leaving others unchanged. A transparent glass floor in a high-rise elevator, for example, creates a perceptual conflict between the visual signal (open void below) and the proprioceptive signal (solid support underfoot), generating a fear response that the designer may intend as spectacle or may need to mitigate.

The paper positions famous architects as case studies of material signature. Frank Gehry's work is recognized partly through his signature use of metals — titanium at Bilbao, stainless steel at Disney Concert Hall — where the reflective, rippling surface becomes an identity marker. Alvar Aalto is associated with brick and wood, Kengo Kuma with wood lattice, Zaha Hadid with composite fibers and parametric curves in concrete, and Oscar Niemeyer with poured concrete in sweeping organic forms. The argument is that these architects did not merely select materials for structural or economic reasons; they selected and manipulated materials to establish a perceptual language that viewers learn to associate with a design philosophy.

For architects working with constrained budgets or sustainability mandates, the framework suggests that material perception can be upgraded without upgrading the material itself. A concrete wall can be board-formed to carry the grain pattern and visual warmth of timber at a fraction of the cost and environmental impact of actual timber cladding. A recycled plastic panel can be textured to read as stone. The design question shifts from "which material should I specify?" to "which perceptual signature do I want the user to receive, and which base material most efficiently delivers it?" This is a specific, actionable reframing: a workplace designer choosing lobby finishes should evaluate candidate materials not only on cost, durability, and sustainability but on the sensory match or deliberate mismatch between their visual and tactile signatures.

The paper's main limitation is that it lacks empirical validation. The perceptual claims — about conflict, warmth, deception, surprise — are derived from architectural observation and general perception theory, not from controlled experiments measuring user responses to specific material pairings. No participants were tested. No effect sizes or confidence intervals are reported. The framework is plausible and internally coherent, but it remains a theoretical proposal awaiting experimental scrutiny.

Would a within-subjects experiment — presenting participants with identical room geometries finished in four material conditions (genuine wood, wood-textured ceramic, wood-printed laminate, and unfinished concrete) while measuring warmth ratings, approach behavior, and skin conductance — reveal whether the perceptual conflict between visual and tactile signals produces measurable discomfort, or whether vision dominates so thoroughly that tactile mismatch goes unnoticed?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0825.pdf

PDF-0826.title: PDF-0826: PNAS paper demonstrating that 3D shape plays a decisive role in the perception of surface opacity. V
PDF-0826.text:
# PDF-0826: PNAS paper demonstrating that 3D shape plays a decisive role in the perception of surface opacity. V

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
PNAS paper demonstrating that 3D shape plays a decisive role in the perception of surface opacity. Visual system uses covariation of local surface orientation and intensity as a cue to opacity vs translucency. Three key findings: (i) identical luminance gradients can appear either translucent or opaque depending on perceived 3D surface orientation; (ii) illusory percepts of translucency can be induced by embedding opaque surfaces in diffuse light fields eliminating orientation-intensity covariation; (iii) illusory opacity can be generated when transparent materials are embedded in light fields where surface orientation and intensity covary. Most significant information used to compute perceived opacity and translucency of surfaces arises at a level of representation where 3D shape is made explicit

## Methods & Sample
- **N** = 14
- **Design**: within_subjects
- **Stimuli/Setting**: Perception and misperception of surface opacity
Phillip J. Marlowa,1, Juno Kimb, and Barton L. Andersona,1
aSchool of Psychology, University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Sydney, Australia; and bSchool of Opto

## Key Statistics
- **p-value**: 0.01
- **Effect size**: None (not_reported)
- **Test**: Ganzfeld translucency F(1,4)=1005 P<<0.01; F(1,4)=576 P<<0.01 specular; F(1,4)=1931 P<<0.01 specular param; subsurface density F(1,8)=30 P<0.01; cylinder translucency misperception F(1,8)=18.7 P<0.01; snake vs cylinder all surfaces P<0.01

## Paper Type
narrative_review

Comprehensive review on translucency perception published in Journal of Vision (2021). Translucency is an optical and perceptual phenomenon characterized by subsurface light transport through objects and materials. It is among the most essential visual attributes of appearance alongside color, gloss, and texture, yet remains the least studied. Knowledge about the visual mechanisms of translucency perception remains limited. Reviews current knowledge gaps, fundamental challenges, applications of translucency perception research, and existing ambiguities with goal to facilitate future research

PDF-0828.title: Material perception, affordances, and sustainability: A mini review
PDF-0828.authors: Strappini, F., Fagioli, S., Mastandrea, S. & Scorolli, C.
PDF-0828.text:
Pick up a wooden cup and a plastic cup shaped the same way. Before you sip from either, you already know which feels more natural, which you prefer, and which you would call sustainable. That instant judgment travels through a chain of perceptual processing that Francesca Strappini, Sabrina Fagioli, Stefano Mastandrea, and Claudia Scorolli mapped in this 2024 mini review in Frontiers in Psychology: the brain extracts material properties through touch and vision, those properties generate affordances for grasping and using objects, affordances shape aesthetic responses, and aesthetic responses drive willingness to adopt sustainable alternatives.

The review bridges three fields that rarely communicate: psychophysics of material perception, Gibsonian affordance theory, and environmental psychology of sustainability. Its central claim is that these three literatures describe successive links in a single causal chain, and that understanding that chain is essential for designing products and environments that people will actually treat sustainably. A recycled material that scores well on lifecycle assessment but feels cheap or looks artificial may trigger negative affordances and aesthetic rejection, undermining the sustainability goal it was meant to serve.

From psychophysics, the review draws evidence that material properties — roughness, glossiness, compliance, temperature — are processed through supramodal pathways. Vision and touch extract overlapping information about surface texture, and the brain integrates these signals to form a unified material percept. Purpura and colleagues (2018) showed that infants develop cross-modal material perception after age three, suggesting a developmental window during which the link between visual and tactile material cues is calibrated. In adults, visual-haptic transfer is reliable: a surface that looks rough also feels rough, and this cross-modal match shapes how people grip, lift, and use objects. Ingvarsdottir and Balkenius (2020) demonstrated that surface glossiness affects grip force scaling — glossy objects are gripped more tightly because the visual signal predicts lower friction — showing that material perception has direct behavioral consequences beyond aesthetic judgment.

The high-level visual system categorizes materials rapidly despite visual ambiguity. In recognition tasks using real-world images, participants identified material categories (wood, metal, fabric, plastic, stone) with high accuracy and speed, even when surface properties were partially occluded or atypically lit. This rapid categorization feeds forward into the affordance system: the moment you perceive a surface as "wood," a cascade of affordances — warmth, grippability, naturalness, sustainability — activates automatically.

From affordance theory, the review shows that perceived material properties shape environmental behavior. Bjelkemyr and colleagues (2015) found that people who perceived materials as environmentally friendly were more likely to recycle them. Thundathil and colleagues (2023) showed that biobased composites that both looked and felt sustainable received higher ratings for beauty, naturalness, and value. The tactile quality of a recycled surface shapes whether consumers perceive the product as sustainably valuable or cheaply made — a distinction with direct implications for whether they adopt or reject sustainable alternatives.

The emotional dimension adds a complication. Bahrudin and Aurisicchio (2018) found that people evaluating sustainable materials experienced both positive emotions (surprise, interest, curiosity) and negative ones (disgust at unfamiliar textures, distrust of novel substrates). When sustainability was framed through a lifecycle narrative — the story of how a bamboo panel grew, was harvested, and was processed — positive emotions dominated and willingness to use the material increased. When sustainability was communicated through technical data (tensile strength, carbon footprint numbers), the emotional response was flat. Telling the story of a material may work better than listing its specifications.

The review proposes a perception-to-sustainability model with four links: material properties → affordances → aesthetic evaluation → sustainable behavior. Each link is supported by independent evidence, but the full chain has not been tested as a unified model. No experiment has shown that manipulating material affordances changes actual purchasing or recycling behavior — only that perception and self-reported intention correlate. The model is theoretically coherent but empirically incomplete.

For architects and product designers, the practical message is specific: sustainability cannot be communicated through certification labels alone. The look and feel of a material must signal sustainability through the same perceptual channels that generate aesthetic appreciation. A workplace designer specifying recycled-content wall panels should select panels whose tactile warmth, visual grain, and surface compliance match what occupants already associate with natural and sustainable materials. If the recycled panel feels like plastic, it will be perceived as plastic regardless of its carbon credentials.

The review synthesizes across diverse studies with different methods, participant groups, material types, and cultural contexts. The proposed causal chain remains a hypothesis assembled from correlational and quasi-experimental evidence. The developmental timeline for material perception (calibration by age three) comes from a small number of infant studies and has not been replicated across cultures with different material environments.

Would a retail experiment — presenting identical products in packaging made from materials with matched versus mismatched visual and tactile sustainability cues, randomized across real store shelves, and tracking actual purchase rates over four weeks with and without opportunities for tactile contact — test whether the perception-affordance-sustainability chain drives behavior through touch as the review predicts?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0828.pdf

PDF-0829.title: Memoryscapes: Audio walks, sense of place, and the integration of art and geography
PDF-0829.text:
Put on headphones, step outside, and press play. A voice in your ears begins narrating the history of the street you are standing on — who lived here in 1940, what the corner shop sold, how the bomb crater across the road was filled in and planted over. As you walk, the narrative adjusts to your location: pass the old church and you hear recordings of parishioners remembering the bells. Turn into the side alley and the artist's voice drops to a whisper, describing what lovers and smugglers once did in this exact passage. The buildings have not changed, but the place has transformed. This is the audio walk, and Toby Butler's 2007 review of the memoryscape concept examines how these site-specific listening experiences deepen sense of place by integrating art, oral history, and cultural geography into the act of walking through space.

Audio walks layer site-specific sound, narrative, and music over physical environments, creating multisensory experiences that transform perception of ordinary places. Unlike guided tours that point out architectural features from the outside, audio walks create an interior experience of exterior space — the listener's attention is directed inward by the narration while their body moves through a real landscape, producing a doubled awareness that Butler argues activates memory, imagination, and embodied spatial awareness beyond what visual experience alone provides.

Butler traces the cultural genealogy of the form from several sources. Soundwalks — structured walks with explicit listening instruction and guidance to sonically interesting places — evolved from the acoustic ecology movement of the 1970s. In a soundwalk, the urban soundscape becomes the focal point: participants are guided to listen to the layered acoustics of a place (traffic rhythms, birdsong, mechanical hum, conversation fragments, wind against building surfaces) rather than looking. The attention shift from visual to auditory transforms the perception of familiar environments by foregrounding the acoustic dimension that occupants normally suppress.

Audio art installations took the form further. Moving audio experiences outside museum and gallery settings meant that cultural performance occurred in geographical, outdoor context — the work was inseparable from the site. Butler cites John Cage's performance of 4'33", in which the performer produces no intentional sound, as a conceptual precursor: the concert hall transforms from a ritualized controlled acoustic space into a dynamic, complex place where the ambient sounds of the audience, the building, and the city become the composition. The audio walk extends this principle by making the entire city audible as a composition — with the walker as both audience and performer.

The memoryscape concept integrates these practices with oral history methodology. In Butler's formulation, a memoryscape is a place that has been enriched with recorded personal narratives, historical testimony, archival sound, and ambient field recordings, all indexed to specific geographical coordinates. As the walker moves through the physical space, the headphone soundtrack delivers location-triggered memories that create a temporal layering: the present-tense experience of the physical environment is overlaid with the past-tense voices of people who occupied the same space decades earlier. The result is a deepened sense of place — a perception that space is not merely geometrical or functional but saturated with human meaning, accumulated through time.

The paper examines specific design techniques that make audio walks effective. Spatial precision matters: the most powerful moments occur when the narrator describes exactly what the listener can see ("the window on the second floor, the one with the cracked frame — that was Doris's room"). Acoustic congruence amplifies the effect: when the recorded soundtrack includes environmental sounds that match the current acoustic environment (traffic, bells, rain), the boundary between past and present blurs. Pacing — walking speed, pause points, duration — determines whether the experience feels meditative or rushed. The best audio walks are designed as architectural experiences: they have rhythm, sequence, climax, and resolution, using the spatial structure of the route as a narrative framework.

For architects and urban designers, the memoryscape concept reframes the relationship between space and meaning. Buildings and streets are not merely physical containers; they are repositories of accumulated experience that can be made perceptually available through sound design. A hospital corridor that plays patient testimonies at specific locations. A university campus walk that layers founding narratives over the current landscape. A residential development that preserves the acoustic memories of the community it displaced. These applications treat sound as a design medium for place-making — not background music or wayfiniding chimes, but structured narratives that connect physical space to human experience.

The paper is a theoretical and cultural review, not an empirical study. Butler does not measure the psychological effects of audio walks (stress reduction, place attachment, wayfinding improvement) or compare them with other placemaking interventions. The examples are drawn primarily from British and American cultural geography; whether the memoryscape form works differently in non-Western or non-English-speaking contexts is not addressed. The relationship between the audio walk experience and longer-term changes in place perception or spatial behavior is hypothesized but not tracked.

Would a controlled study — assigning residents to experience a memoryscape audio walk of their neighborhood versus a conventional guided tour versus an unguided walk, then measuring place attachment, neighborhood satisfaction, and spatial memory at one week and three months — show that auditory narrative layering produces measurable and persistent changes in how people bond with place?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0829.pdf

PDF-0831.title: Indoor acoustics for autism-friendly spaces require lower reverberation and reduced background noise
PDF-0831.text:
For people with autism spectrum conditions, the acoustic environment is not background — it is foreground. Federica Bettarelli and colleagues review the indoor acoustic requirements for autism-friendly spaces in this 2021 study in *Applied Sciences*, synthesizing evidence on how sensory processing differences in autism interact with architectural acoustics.

Autistic individuals frequently report heightened sensitivity to sound. Sounds that neurotypical individuals process as background (HVAC hum, conversation murmur, fluorescent lighting buzz) can be experienced as intrusive, distracting, and even painful. This sensory difference means that acoustic design standards developed for neurotypical populations are insufficient for spaces serving autistic users.

The review identifies specific acoustic parameters that must be addressed. Reverberation time should be lower than standard recommendations — shorter than 0.5 seconds in occupied conditions, compared to the 0.6–0.8 seconds typical of educational and institutional spaces. Excess reverberation makes speech less intelligible and increases the overall noise level, both of which are particularly problematic for individuals with auditory processing sensitivities.

Background noise levels should be lower than standard targets — below 30 dB(A) in learning spaces, compared to the 35–40 dB(A) typically permitted. The contribution of mechanical systems (HVAC) is particularly problematic because it is continuous, broadband, and unavoidable. Lowering HVAC-generated noise requires equipment selection, duct design, and vibration isolation measures that exceed standard practice.

Sound insulation between spaces should exceed standard specifications. Impact noise from footsteps, chair scraping, and equipment is often more disturbing than airborne noise for sound-sensitive individuals. Separating loud activities (gymnasiums, music rooms, cafeterias) from quiet activities (classrooms, therapy rooms, quiet spaces) through spatial planning — not just partition construction — is essential.

For architects designing schools, therapy centers, residential facilities, and public buildings intended to include autistic users, the review provides specific, actionable acoustic targets that differ from standard practice. Autism-friendly acoustic design is not a specialized luxury — it serves the estimated 1–2% of the population on the autism spectrum, along with the many more individuals with subclinical sound sensitivity.

The review synthesizes a small evidence base — few controlled studies measure acoustic design effects specifically in autistic populations. Many recommendations are extrapolated from general noise sensitivity research or from clinical reports rather than from direct experimental evidence.

What controlled study — varying reverberation time and background noise level in a classroom used by autistic students while measuring on-task behavior, learning outcomes, and physiological stress — could establish the optimal acoustic parameters for autism-friendly educational spaces?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0831.pdf

PDF-0834.title: PDF-0834: Wu et al. (2023) — Soundscape, Well-being and Mental Health During/After COVID-19
PDF-0834.text:
# PDF-0834: Wu et al. (2023) — Soundscape, Well-being and Mental Health During/After COVID-19

## Paper Type
Editorial introducing a Frontiers Research Topic (7 articles by 27 authors)

## Core Argument
The COVID-19 pandemic—through strict lockdowns and behavioral restrictions—profoundly altered urban acoustic environments worldwide. This editorial synthesizes seven contributions showing how these soundscape changes affected residents' mental health, sound perception, and wellbeing, while also revealing the restorative potential of positive soundscapes.

## Key Themes From the Research Topic
1. **Acoustic monitoring during lockdown**: Yang & Kang measured A-weighted sound pressure levels (LAeq) across all 14 floors of an urban apartment building, documenting measurable changes in environmental noise before vs. during vs. after lockdown.
2. **Hospital soundscapes**: Park et al. found hospital patients and staff were especially vulnerable to emotional disturbance during COVID-19; satisfaction with indoor acoustic environment and odor significantly predicted perceived indoor environment quality during recovery.
3. **Ideal indoor soundscape**: Torresin et al. surveyed 464 London residents during lockdown, finding the ideal indoor soundscape was described as quiet, well-insulated, with access to natural sounds, music, and urban background—fostering privacy, intimacy, and freedom from noise constraints.
4. **Environmental perception under traffic noise**: Ren et al. used five indicators (willingness to walk, relaxation, safety, beauty, comfort) to measure environmental health of pedestrian streets.
5. **Sound-light cross-modal interaction**: Yu et al. studied how sound and light interact at night in residential settings, finding visual elements can change sound perception but tolerance limits are not reduced by superposition of two intrusive variables.
6. **Elderly dining comfort**: Mu & Kang examined dining facility soundscapes in elderly care, comparing before/after pandemic conditions.
7. **Aircraft noise & natural sounds**: Qu et al. identified moderating effects of subjective factors and restorative effects of natural sounds on aircraft noise evaluation.

## Environmental Context
Urban residential, hospital, pedestrian street, elderly care facility, and residential nighttime environments during and after COVID-19 lockdowns.

## Visual Content
- No figures, tables, or graphs in this editorial (2 pages)

## Design Implications
Soundscape research during COVID-19 highlights the importance of designing built environments with restorative acoustic qualities—natural sounds, adequate insulation, and control over noise intrusion—to protect mental health during crises and normal conditions alike.

## Key References
- Zhou et al. (2020) hospital ward acoustics and psychological indices
- Jiang et al. (2022) ten questions on soundscape valuation
- Liu et al. (2022) noise definition in humanitarian contexts
- Alonso et al. (2022) housing design and wellbeing during COVID-19
- Ge et al. (2023) natural sounds and anxiety disorders

PDF-0836.title: Urban street tree canopy reduces heat stress and increases pedestrian thermal comfort
PDF-0836.text:
A mature street tree can reduce the perceived temperature on a summer sidewalk by several degrees — a thermal intervention that no mechanical cooling system can replicate outdoors. This 2021 study in *Landscape and Urban Planning* quantified the microclimate benefits of urban street tree canopy, measuring both physical thermal variables and pedestrian thermal comfort under varying canopy conditions.

The research combined microclimate measurement (air temperature, mean radiant temperature, humidity, wind speed) with thermal comfort assessment (Universal Thermal Climate Index — UTCI) and pedestrian perception surveys in urban streets with systematically varying tree canopy cover. Sites ranged from treeless streets with full solar exposure to streets with dense canopy providing near-complete shade.

The physical measurements confirmed large microclimate effects. Under dense tree canopy, mean radiant temperature (MRT) — the dominant variable for outdoor thermal comfort — was reduced by 10–20°C compared to fully exposed locations. Air temperature reductions were more modest (1–3°C) but consistent. The UTCI reduction under canopy translated from "strong heat stress" to "moderate heat stress" or "no thermal stress" — a perceptually and physiologically meaningful shift.

Pedestrian thermal comfort perception tracked the physical measurements closely. Participants under tree canopy rated their thermal comfort as significantly better and were substantially more willing to linger in the space. The behavioral consequence: shaded streets attracted more pedestrians, slower walking speeds, and longer dwell times than exposed streets, producing the social vitality that urban planners aim for.

The tree species and canopy characteristics mattered. Dense, broadleaf canopy with low crown height and wide spread provided the most effective shading. Sparse canopy with small leaves and open structure provided less MRT reduction. Deciduous species that shed leaves in winter allowed solar access when warming is desirable — a seasonal adaptation that no fixed shading structure can provide.

For urban designers, the finding quantifies the thermal-comfort business case for street trees. In summer-dominant climates, tree canopy is the most effective available strategy for making streets walkable and livable during heat events — a public health intervention as much as an aesthetic one.

The study is observational and site-specific — the microclimate benefits of trees vary with climate, urban geometry, and wind patterns. The thermal comfort measurements represent daytime summer conditions; nighttime and winter effects differ. Long-term maintenance, irrigation, and growth requirements are not addressed.

What multi-city comparative study — measuring microclimate, pedestrian use, and heat-related health outcomes on matched streets with and without mature tree canopy across different climate zones — could generalize the thermal-comfort benefit of urban trees and identify the canopy coverage threshold for meaningful health protection?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0836.pdf

PDF-0840.title: Classroom acoustic design predicts speech intelligibility — and children are more vulnerable than adults
PDF-0840.text:
Children cannot hear as well as adults in noisy rooms — not because their ears are worse, but because their brains are less skilled at extracting speech from noise. This 2024 study in *Building Acoustics* examined the relationship between classroom acoustic design and speech intelligibility, demonstrating that children require better acoustic conditions than adults to achieve the same level of speech understanding.

Speech intelligibility depends on three acoustic parameters that the classroom architect can control: reverberation time, background noise level, and the speech-to-noise ratio at the listener's ear. In adult listeners, speech remains intelligible at signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) as low as +3 to +5 dB. Children require SNRs of +10 to +15 dB — meaning their listening environment must be significantly quieter relative to the speech signal for them to understand the teacher's words.

The study measured acoustic conditions in classrooms and correlated them with speech intelligibility test scores for child listeners. Classrooms with reverberation times below 0.6 seconds and background noise levels below 35 dB(A) provided adequate intelligibility for most children. Classrooms that exceeded these thresholds — common in buildings with hard parallel walls, high ceilings, and inadequate acoustic treatment — produced measurable intelligibility deficits.

The physiological explanation: children's auditory processing systems are still developing. The neural mechanisms for segregating speech from competing sounds (auditory stream segregation) mature through childhood and adolescence. Until mature, these mechanisms require a cleaner acoustic signal to function effectively — meaning that the acoustic environment must compensate for the developing auditory system.

For school architects, the finding specifies acoustic design as a learning-outcome variable. A classroom that meets adult acoustic standards may fail to provide adequate intelligibility for its actual users — children. The design targets must be child-appropriate: shorter reverberation times, lower background noise, better sound insulation from adjacent spaces, and classroom geometry that reinforces the teacher's voice relative to background noise.

The study uses controlled listening tests with standardized speech material — real classroom listening involves dynamic speech, contextual cues, and visual lip-reading that supplemented by purely auditory intelligibility measures. The children tested may not represent the full range of auditory processing abilities in typical classrooms (second-language learners, children with mild hearing loss, children with attention deficits).

What intervention study — installing acoustic treatment (absorptive ceiling panels, wall-mounted absorbers) in matched classrooms and measuring both speech intelligibility scores and academic performance before and after the intervention — could quantify the educational return on investment of classroom acoustic improvement?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0840.pdf

# PDF-0842: Kumar et al. (2017, Current Biology) establish the brain basis for misophonia using fMRI. Key findin

## Paper Type
empirical_research (between_groups)

## Core Finding
Kumar et al. (2017, Current Biology) establish the brain basis for misophonia using fMRI. Key findings: (1) trigger sounds elicit exaggerated response in anterior insula, (2) abnormal functional connectivity of anterior insula exists in misophonia, (3) heightened autonomic responses mediated by anterior insula, (4) misophonia is associated with altered interoception. The anterior insula — critical for interoceptive signals and emotion processing — shows both structural and functional abnormalities in misophonia. This is a landmark paper establishing the neural mechanism underlying an extreme form of sound sensitivity relevant to built environment design

# PDF-0843: Taghipour et al. (2020, Frontiers in Psychology) investigate room acoustical parameters as predictor

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
Taghipour et al. (2020, Frontiers in Psychology) investigate room acoustical parameters as predictors of acoustic comfort in outdoor housing complex spaces. Three laboratory experiments using ODEON virtual yard simulation with 2D spatialization over five loudspeakers. Moderate facade absorption increases acoustic comfort. Pleasant sounds → comfort, unpleasant → discomfort. Lower comfort ratings at positions with high SPL or strong flutter echoes. STI, D50, C50/C80, EDT, and LF80 significantly correlated with acoustic comfort. Linear mixed-effect models with LAeq as significant predictor of acoustic comfort established

- **N** = 42
- **Design**: within_subjects

PDF-0844.title: PDF-0844
PDF-0844.text:
Most people believe that a row of trees along a highway blocks traffic noise. They are partly right and mostly wrong. Fan Yang, Zhi Yi Bao, and Zhu Jun Zhu at Zhejiang University showed in this 2011 study in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health that landscape plants reduce psychological noise even when their physical sound dampening is negligible, and that this perceptual shift shows up in brain activity.

Typical urban vegetation strips cut only 1 to 3 decibels of sound per 30 meters of depth. Humans perceive a halving of loudness at about 10 to 15 dB of reduction. So the physical effect of most planted buffers is far too small to notice by ear. Yet ninety percent of survey respondents in this study believed that plants reduce noise, and fifty-five percent overestimated the plants' actual dampening power. The gap between perceived and actual noise reduction suggests that the primary mechanism is psychological: seeing vegetation changes how the brain processes the noise.

The team combined two approaches. First, a questionnaire survey captured public beliefs about landscape noise dampening. Second, an EEG experiment recorded brain activity in forty participants who watched two types of standardized video through video glasses: one set showed traffic scenes (roads, vehicles, urban infrastructure) and the other showed vegetation scenes (parks, planted corridors, green spaces). Both video sets played at identical ambient sound levels. The EEG recorded from sixteen scalp electrodes and compared power across six frequency bands: delta, theta, alpha-1, alpha-2, beta-1, and beta-2.

The central finding was a clear split in brain activity between conditions. Beta-1 and beta-2 power at frontal, frontopolar, occipital, and central electrode sites (F3, F4, F7, F8, Fp1, Fp2, O1, O2) increased significantly more in the traffic group than in the vegetation group (p < 0.05). Beta activity tracks active cognitive processing, alertness, and arousal. Alpha power was significantly lower in the vegetation group at frontopolar, central, parietal, and occipital regions, consistent with prior work linking reduced alpha to relaxation. The right hemisphere showed more emotional activity than the left across all frequency bands.

A single-factor ANOVA on the relative percent change in EEG power showed significant differences between conditions for alpha and beta bands at multiple electrode sites. Theta band differences did not reach significance.

For landscape architects and urban planners, the study provides brain-level evidence for planting vegetation buffers along noise corridors. Even when the plants do not measurably reduce sound pressure, they shift brain activity from high-arousal patterns (beta-dominant, right-lateralized negative affect) toward lower-arousal patterns (reduced alpha, more balanced hemisphere activation). Visual screening may matter more than acoustic screening for noise-adjacent housing and office development.

The EEG experiment used video glasses, not immersive real environments. Forty participants watched standardized clips, not actual traffic and vegetation settings. EEG has strong temporal resolution but weak spatial resolution, making it hard to pinpoint where in the brain the power changes originate. The study did not measure actual sound levels from the depicted vegetation. No self-report measures of perceived noise accompanied the EEG data, so the link between brain changes and subjective noise perception is inferred, not measured.

Would residents of apartments facing highway vegetation buffers show different EEG resting-state profiles compared to residents of identical apartments facing unscreened highways at the same measured sound levels? An ambulatory EEG study comparing frontopolar beta power and alpha asymmetry in matched pairs of residents would test whether this laboratory finding transfers to chronic residential exposure.

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0844.pdf

PDF-0845.title: Noise pollution damages cardiovascular and cognitive health — not just hearing
PDF-0845.text:
Noise is the pollutant you cannot see, cannot smell, and cannot close a door against — sound travels through walls, around corners, and through the gaps in every window seal. Walihabib Hemmat, Hesam, and Atifnigar, in a 2023 comprehensive review, trace the pathways through which chronic noise exposure damages human health far beyond the obvious target of the auditory system.

The cardiovascular pathway is the most thoroughly documented. Chronic exposure to environmental noise — especially road traffic noise above 55 dB(A) during the day and 45 dB(A) at night — triggers sustained activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and the sympathetic nervous system. The stress hormones released (cortisol, adrenaline, noradrenaline) elevate blood pressure, increase heart rate, and promote systemic inflammation. Large epidemiological studies have linked long-term traffic noise exposure to increased incidence of hypertension, ischemic heart disease, stroke, and myocardial infarction. The World Health Organization estimates that environmental noise in Western Europe alone causes 12,000 premature deaths annually and 48,000 new cases of ischemic heart disease per year.

The cognitive pathway operates through both chronic stress and direct interference. Noise disrupts working memory, sustained attention, reading comprehension, and executive function. In children, the evidence is particularly concerning: studies in schools near airports (the RANCH study across multiple European countries) found that chronic aircraft noise exposure impaired reading comprehension and long-term memory, with no evidence of habituation over time. Children did not adapt to the noise — their cognitive performance remained impaired for as long as the exposure continued.

The sleep disruption pathway compounds both cardiovascular and cognitive effects. Nighttime noise above 45 dB(A) fragments sleep architecture, reducing slow-wave sleep and REM duration. The consequences cascade: fragmented sleep impairs next-day cognitive performance, emotional regulation, and immune function, while simultaneously elevating inflammatory markers that contribute to cardiovascular risk. A single noise event above 55 dB(A) can trigger a cortical arousal without waking the sleeper — the brain registers the threat even during sleep.

The review catalogues mitigation strategies at three scales. At the source: quieter pavement surfaces, traffic speed reduction (noise energy scales roughly with the cube of vehicle speed), engine and tire noise regulations, and the shift to electric vehicles (which reduce engine noise but not tire noise above 30 km/h). At the propagation path: noise barriers, building setbacks, green buffers (vegetation strips attenuate high-frequency noise by 2–5 dB per 10 meters), and urban canyon geometry that redirects sound upward rather than across the street. At the receiver: façade insulation (double-glazed windows, sealed building envelopes), sound-absorbing interior materials, and active noise cancellation in critical spaces.

For architects, the implication is that acoustic design is health design. Specifying a building envelope is simultaneously specifying the cardiovascular and cognitive load its occupants will carry. A bedroom window that faces a street carrying 65 dB(A) traffic noise — common in most cities — exposes its occupant to a noise level the WHO classifies as associated with significant health risk. The architect who specifies that window's acoustic rating is making a public health decision.

The review is narrative, not systematic — it surveys the landscape without applying formal inclusion/exclusion criteria or quantitative synthesis. Effect sizes are rarely reported. The causal pathway from noise to cardiovascular disease is supported by strong epidemiological evidence but few randomized controlled trials (for obvious ethical reasons). Individual variation in noise sensitivity — which may have a genetic component — is acknowledged but not modeled.

What longitudinal study — measuring cardiovascular biomarkers, cognitive performance, and sleep architecture in residents before and after a municipality installs quiet asphalt and reduces speed limits on their street — could quantify the integrated health benefit of a single infrastructure intervention?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0845.pdf

# PDF-0846: Vardaxis, Bard & Persson Waye (2018, Building Acoustics) review studies that approached acoustic com

## Paper Type
systematic_review

## Core Argument
Vardaxis, Bard & Persson Waye (2018, Building Acoustics) review studies that approached acoustic comfort through association of objective field data and subjective responses, evaluated using Bradford Hill criteria. Acoustic comfort requires more than low noise or absence of annoyance — it should include human perception. Current standardized methods for assessing dwellings do not represent comfort. Noise is a significant issue in living environments especially for impact noise. Extended low-frequency spectra down to 50 Hz suggested for impact measurements. Lightweight structures perform inefficiently compared to heavyweight for low-frequency transmission

PDF-0848.text:
# PDF-0848: Klatte, Lachmann & Meis (2010, Noise & Health) examine effects of noise and reverberation on speech 

## Paper Type
empirical_research (between_groups)

## Core Finding
Klatte, Lachmann & Meis (2010, Noise & Health) examine effects of noise and reverberation on speech perception and comprehension in children vs adults. Children were more impaired than adults by background sounds in both speech perception and listening comprehension. Classroom noise evoked reliable disruption in children even under short reverberation. RT had no effect on speech perception in silence but severely increased impairments due to background sounds. Background speech had stronger effect on listening comprehension than classroom noise, remaining significant when speech perception was controlled — indicating higher-order cognitive process involvement. Children's self-ratings of disturbance were low and uncorrelated with actual disruption — children did not consciously realize the detrimental effects

- **Design**: between_groups
- **Stimuli/Setting**: Noise & Health, October-December 2010, Volume 12

PDF-0850.title: Bright light effects on psychophysiology depend on time of day — morning light boosts, evening light disrupts
PDF-0850.text:
The same bright light can be beneficial or harmful — depending on when you receive it. This study compared the psychophysiological effects of bright light exposure delivered at different times of day, finding that morning bright light improved alertness, mood, and cognitive performance, while evening bright light suppressed melatonin, delayed sleep onset, and impaired next-morning function — even though the light stimulus was physically identical.

The experiment used a within-subjects design where participants experienced the same bright light exposure (above 2500 lux) during either the morning, afternoon, or evening on separate experimental days. Salivary melatonin, subjective alertness, mood ratings, and cognitive performance (reaction time, working memory) were measured at multiple timepoints.

Morning bright light produced the most positive outcomes: earlier melatonin offset (confirming circadian phase advance), increased daytime alertness, improved mood, and faster reaction times. These benefits are consistent with the circadian biology of light: morning exposure reinforces the circadian wake signal, promoting consolidated wakefulness throughout the day.

Afternoon bright light produced modest benefits on alertness without significant circadian disruption — the circadian system is relatively less sensitive to light exposure during the biological day.

Evening bright light produced the expected negative chronobiological effects: delayed melatonin onset (shifting the circadian clock later), prolonged sleep latency, reduced sleep quality, and impaired next-morning alertness and performance. The evening exposure delayed the biological night, making it harder to fall asleep and producing less restorative sleep.

For architects and lighting designers, the time-of-day dependency has a direct architectural implication: the same lighting specification that is health-promoting in the morning becomes health-damaging in the evening. A building that delivers bright, blue-enriched light uniformly throughout the day is exposing occupants to the right light at the wrong time during evening hours. The prescription is spectral and temporal: bright and cool in the morning, moderate and neutral at midday, dim and warm in the evening.

The study used controlled laboratory conditions. Real-world light exposure is continuous and variable, not discrete; ocular light history (the cumulative light received over preceding hours and days) modulates the acute response to any given light exposure. Individual differences in chronotype produce substantial variation in the timing of optimal and harmful light exposure.

What field study — deploying automatically tuning lighting systems in residential settings, programmed to deliver bright cool light in the morning and dim warm light in the evening, while measuring actigraphy sleep quality and morning performance — could quantify the health benefit of time-of-day-appropriate residential lighting?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0850.pdf

# PDF-0851: Scheer, van Doornen & Buijs (1999, J Biological Rhythms) demonstrate that light and the diurnal cycl

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
Scheer, van Doornen & Buijs (1999, J Biological Rhythms) demonstrate that light and the diurnal cycle affect human heart rate. Two experiments: Experiment 1 measured HR at different times over the day-night cycle at 0 lux and indoor light; Experiment 2 measured HR at controlled intensities (0, 100, 800 lux) at different times. Clear diurnal rhythm in resting HR demonstrated (similar to constant routine conditions). Light increases resting HR depending on phase of day-night cycle and intensity. Results strongly suggest that the circadian pacemaker (SCN) modulates human heart rate. SCN influences extend beyond circadian entrainment to direct cardiovascular regulation

- **Design**: within_subjects

# PDF-0852: Ikei, Song & Miyazaki (2017, IJERPH) examine physiological effects of touching coated wood. N=18 fem

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
Ikei, Song & Miyazaki (2017, IJERPH) examine physiological effects of touching coated wood. N=18 female university students (21.7±1.6 yr). Tactile stimulation with uncoated wood calmed prefrontal cortex activity vs urethane/mirror finish, increased parasympathetic nervous activity vs vitreous/urethane/mirror finish, decreased heart rate vs mirror finish. Oil- and vitreous-finished wood also calmed left prefrontal cortex and decreased HR relative to mirror-finished wood. Demonstrates physiological relaxation response from touching natural/lightly-coated wood — direct material-touch sensory pathway to autonomic regulation

# PDF-0853: Ikei, Song & Miyazaki (2017, IJERPH) clarify physiological effects of touching wood vs other materia

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
Ikei, Song & Miyazaki (2017, IJERPH) clarify physiological effects of touching wood vs other materials. N=18 female students (21.7±1.6 yr). Touching white oak significantly (1) decreased oxy-Hb in left/right prefrontal cortex relative to marble, tile, and steel, and (2) increased ln(HF) parasympathetic activity relative to marble and steel. Touching wood calms prefrontal cortex and induces parasympathetic nervous activity more than other building materials — inducing physiological relaxation. Foundation for material selection in architecture and interior design

# PDF-0855: Schreiner et al. (2020, Wood Science and Technology) investigate psychophysiological responses to wo

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
Schreiner et al. (2020, Wood Science and Technology) investigate psychophysiological responses to wood odors. Subjects smelled pine needles oil, cedar wood oil, α-pinene, and thymoquinone and evaluated odor percept and subjective feelings while HR, finger temp, and skin conductance were monitored. Wood odors were distinct in pleasantness, familiarity, and intensity. All odors enhanced subjective happiness and contentment. No odor-specific physiological effects became evident, but certain physiological measures correlated with self-reports of happy and relaxed feelings and intensity ratings. Provides basis for wood odors as ambient scents in built environments

# PDF-0856: Chellappa, Gordijn & Cajochen (2011, Progress in Brain Research Chapter 7) review effects of light o

## Paper Type
narrative_review

## Core Argument
Chellappa, Gordijn & Cajochen (2011, Progress in Brain Research Chapter 7) review effects of light on cognition and sleep. Light elicits robust nonvisual effects on physiological and behavioral variables and cognitive performance. Effects crucially rely on properties: dose, duration, timing, wavelength. fMRI studies reveal how light optimizes brain function during sustained attention tasks. Two main issues addressed: (1) how light impinges on cognition via consolidation of sleep-wake cycles, and (2) how light directly impacts sustained attention. Understanding light effects on sleep and cognition can improve light settings at home and work for well-being

PDF-0858.text:
# PDF-0858: Mills, Tomkins & Schlangen (2007, J Circadian Rhythms) prospective controlled intervention in shift-

## Paper Type
empirical_research (between_groups)

## Core Finding
Mills, Tomkins & Schlangen (2007, J Circadian Rhythms) prospective controlled intervention in shift-working call centre. 14-week study, two floors (intervention vs control). 17000K fluorescent lighting significantly improved self-reported concentration: mean score improved 36.8% (intervention) vs 1.7% (control), p<0.05. Substantial within-group improvements in fatigue (26.9%), alertness (28.2%), daytime sleepiness (31%), work performance (19.4%) as assessed by Columbia Jet Lag Scale, and vitality (28.4%) and mental health (13.9%) per SF-36. Improvements occurred mostly within first 7 weeks. Non-image-forming retinal receptors provide anatomical basis for these non-visual biological effects of light

## Paper Type
empirical_research (within_subjects)

## Core Finding
Vandewalle et al. (2006, Current Biology) demonstrate that daytime light exposure dynamically enhances brain responses. fMRI (n=12 subjects showing alerting effect plus nonresponders). Light-induced improvement in subjective alertness linearly related to posterior thalamus responses. Light enhanced responses in cortical areas supporting attentional oddball effects, preventing decreases of activity otherwise observed during darkness. Responses to light were remarkably dynamic — declined within minutes after light ended following region-specific time courses. Subcortical alertness structures modulate cortical activity in networks for ongoing nonvisual cognitive processes. Non-image-forming (NIF) pathway mediates these effects

PDF-0860.title: Energy behavior change requires social and structural interventions — information alone is insufficient
PDF-0860.text:
Telling people to save energy does not make them save energy. This 2016 study in *Energy Research and Social Science* reviewed the evidence for behavior-change interventions aimed at reducing household and building energy consumption, finding that information-based approaches (knowledge campaigns, energy audits, feedback displays) produce modest, short-lived effects, while structural interventions (default settings, automation, social norms, financial incentives) produce larger and more durable behavior change.

The hierarchy of effectiveness emerged clearly. Structural interventions — changing the default thermostat setting, installing automated lighting controls, redesigning the choice architecture so that the energy-efficient option is the easiest option — produced the largest and most consistent energy savings. These interventions work because they do not require conscious effort, motivation, or attention from the occupant. The building works efficiently because its systems are designed to work efficiently, not because the occupant remembers to turn things off.

Social norm interventions — telling occupants how their energy use compares to their neighbors — produced moderate effects. The psychological mechanism is comparative self-evaluation: people who learn they use more energy than their neighbors reduce consumption, while people who learn they use less than average sometimes increase it (the "boomerang effect"). Coupling social comparison with an injunctive norm ("good neighbors conserve") reduces the boomerang effect.

Information and feedback interventions — energy bills, real-time displays, educational materials — produced the smallest and least durable effects. Awareness of energy use increased, but behavioral change was modest and decayed when feedback was removed. The knowledge-action gap that plagues environmental behavior applies with full force to energy conservation.

For architects and building systems designers, the review reframes energy-efficient design from a specification problem to a behavior problem. A building with efficient systems that requires occupant discipline to achieve its design performance will underperform. A building whose systems default to efficient behavior — occupancy-sensing lighting, scheduled temperature setbacks, automated blind control — will achieve its design performance regardless of occupant attention.

The review draws on a heterogeneous literature with varied interventions, samples, and outcome measures. Effect sizes are difficult to compare across studies. Cultural and climatic contexts moderate intervention effectiveness substantially.

What randomized field trial — deploying either structural, social, or informational energy-saving interventions in matched residential buildings and measuring energy consumption over two years — could provide the definitive head-to-head comparison of behavior change strategies for building energy conservation?

Source: data/pdfs/corpus/PDF-0860.pdf

systematic_review

Bikomeye, Balza & Beyer (2021, IJERPH) systematic review of experimental studies on schoolyard greening impact. 4 databases searched, 1843 articles retrieved, 6 met inclusion criteria (experimental/pre-post design). de Dear & Brager (2002, Energy and Buildings) present the adaptive thermal comfort model that led to revisions of ASHRAE Standard 55. Landmark paper showing that occupants of naturally ventilated buildings have broader thermal comfort tolerance than predicted by PMV/PPD models, because of behavioral, physiological, and psychological adaptation.
